Lo, their good [is] not in their hand: the counsel of the wicked is far from me.
How oft is the candle of the wicked put out! and [how oft] cometh their destruction upon them! [God] distributeth sorrows in his anger.
They are as stubble before the wind, and as chaff that the storm carrieth away.
God layeth up his iniquity for his children: he rewardeth him, and he shall know [it. ]
His eyes shall see his destruction, and he shall drink of the wrath of the Almighty.
For what pleasure [hath] he in his house after him, when the number of his months is cut off in the midst?
Shall [any] teach God knowledge? seeing he judgeth those that are high.
One dieth in his full strength, being wholly at ease and quiet.
His breasts are full of milk, and his bones are moistened with marrow.
And another dieth in the bitterness of his soul, and never eateth with pleasure.
They shall lie down alike in the dust, and the worms shall cover them.
Behold, I know your thoughts, and the devices [which] ye wrongfully imagine against me.
For ye say, Where [is] the house of the prince? and where [are] the dwelling places of the wicked?
Have ye not asked them that go by the way? and do ye not know their tokens,
That the wicked is reserved to the day of destruction? they shall be brought forth to the day of wrath.
Who shall declare his way to his face? and who shall repay him [what] he hath done?
Yet shall he be brought to the grave, and shall remain in the tomb.
The clods of the valley shall be sweet unto him, and every man shall draw after him, as [there are] innumerable before him.
How then comfort ye me in vain, seeing in your answers there remaineth falsehood?
Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said,
Can a man be profitable unto God, as he that is wise may be profitable unto himself?
[Is it] any pleasure to the Almighty, that thou art righteous? or [is it] gain [to him] that thou makest thy ways perfect?
Will he reprove thee for fear of thee? will he enter with thee into judgment?
[Is] not thy wickedness great? and thine iniquities infinite?
For thou hast taken a pledge from thy brother for nought, and stripped the naked of their clothing.
Thou hast not given water to the weary to drink, and thou hast withholden bread from the hungry.
But [as for] the mighty man, he had the earth; and the honourable man dwelt in it.
Thou hast sent widows away empty, and the arms of the fatherless have been broken.
Therefore snares [are] round about thee, and sudden fear troubleth thee;
Or darkness, [that] thou canst not see; and abundance of waters cover thee.
[Is] not God in the height of heaven? and behold the height of the stars, how high they are!
And thou sayest, How doth God know? can he judge through the dark cloud?
Thick clouds [are] a covering to him, that he seeth not; and he walketh in the circuit of heaven.
Hast thou marked the old way which wicked men have trodden?
Which were cut down out of time, whose foundation was overflown with a flood:
Which said unto God, Depart from us: and what can the Almighty do for them?
Yet he filled their houses with good [things:] but the counsel of the wicked is far from me.
The righteous see [it,] and are glad: and the innocent laugh them to scorn.
Whereas our substance is not cut down, but the remnant of them the fire consumeth.
Acquaint now thyself with him, and be at peace: thereby good shall come unto thee.
Receive, I pray thee, the law from his mouth, and lay up his words in thine heart.
If thou return to the Almighty, thou shalt be built up, thou shalt put away iniquity far from thy tabernacles.
Then shalt thou lay up gold as dust, and the [gold] of Ophir as the stones of the brooks.
Yea, the Almighty shall be thy defence, and thou shalt have plenty of silver.
For then shalt thou have thy delight in the Almighty, and shalt lift up thy face unto God.
Thou shalt make thy prayer unto him, and he shall hear thee, and thou shalt pay thy vows.
Thou shalt also decree a thing, and it shall be established unto thee: and the light shall shine upon thy ways.
When [men] are cast down, then thou shalt say, [There is] lifting up; and he shall save the humble person.
He shall deliver the island of the innocent: and it is delivered by the pureness of thine hands.
Then Job answered and said,
Even to day [is] my complaint bitter: my stroke is heavier than my groaning.
Oh that I knew where I might find him! [that] I might come [even] to his seat!
I would order [my] cause before him, and fill my mouth with arguments.
I would know the words [which] he would answer me, and understand what he would say unto me.
Will he plead against me with [his] great power? No; but he would put [strength] in me.
There the righteous might dispute with him; so should I be delivered for ever from my judge.
Behold, I go forward, but he [is] not [there;] and backward, but I cannot perceive him:
On the left hand, where he doth work, but I cannot behold [him:] he hideth himself on the right hand, that I cannot see [him: ]
But he knoweth the way that I take: [when] he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold.
My foot hath held his steps, his way have I kept, and not declined.
Neither have I gone back from the commandment of his lips; I have esteemed the words of his mouth more than my necessary [food. ]
But he [is] in one [mind,] and who can turn him? and [what] his soul desireth, even [that] he doeth.
For he performeth [the thing that is] appointed for me: and many such [things are] with him.
Therefore am I troubled at his presence: when I consider, I am afraid of him.
For God maketh my heart soft, and the Almighty troubleth me:
Because I was not cut off before the darkness, [neither] hath he covered the darkness from my face.
Why, seeing times are not hidden from the Almighty, do they that know him not see his days?
[Some] remove the landmarks; they violently take away flocks, and feed [thereof. ]
They drive away the ass of the fatherless, they take the widow’s ox for a pledge.
They turn the needy out of the way: the poor of the earth hide themselves together.
Behold, [as] wild asses in the desert, go they forth to their work; rising betimes for a prey: the wilderness [yieldeth] food for them [and] for [their] children.
They reap [every one] his corn in the field: and they gather the vintage of the wicked.
They cause the naked to lodge without clothing, that [they have] no covering in the cold.
They are wet with the showers of the mountains, and embrace the rock for want of a shelter.
They pluck the fatherless from the breast, and take a pledge of the poor.
They cause [him] to go naked without clothing, and they take away the sheaf [from] the hungry;
[Which] make oil within their walls, [and] tread [their] winepresses, and suffer thirst.
Men groan from out of the city, and the soul of the wounded crieth out: yet God layeth not folly [to them. ]
They are of those that rebel against the light; they know not the ways thereof, nor abide in the paths thereof.
The murderer rising with the light killeth the poor and needy, and in the night is as a thief.
The eye also of the adulterer waiteth for the twilight, saying, No eye shall see me: and disguiseth [his] face.
In the dark they dig through houses, [which] they had marked for themselves in the daytime: they know not the light.
For the morning [is] to them even as the shadow of death: if [one] know [them, they are in] the terrors of the shadow of death.
He [is] swift as the waters; their portion is cursed in the earth: he beholdeth not the way of the vineyards.
Drought and heat consume the snow waters: [so doth] the grave [those which] have sinned.
The womb shall forget him; the worm shall feed sweetly on him; he shall be no more remembered; and wickedness shall be broken as a tree.
He evil entreateth the barren that [beareth] not: and doeth not good to the widow.
He draweth also the mighty with his power: he riseth up, and no [man] is sure of life.
[Though] it be given him [to be] in safety, whereon he resteth; yet his eyes [are] upon their ways.
They are exalted for a little while, but are gone and brought low; they are taken out of the way as all [other,] and cut off as the tops of the ears of corn.
And if [it be] not [so] now, who will make me a liar, and make my speech nothing worth?
Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said,
Dominion and fear [are] with him, he maketh peace in his high places.
Is there any number of his armies? and upon whom doth not his light arise?
How then can man be justified with God? or how can he be clean [that is] born of a woman?
Behold even to the moon, and it shineth not; yea, the stars are not pure in his sight.
How much less man, [that is] a worm? and the son of man, [which is] a worm?
But Job answered and said,
How hast thou helped [him that is] without power? [how] savest thou the arm [that hath] no strength?
How hast thou counseled [him that hath] no wisdom? and [how] hast thou plentifully declared the thing as it is?
To whom hast thou uttered words? and whose spirit came from thee?
Dead [things] are formed from under the waters, and the inhabitants thereof.
Hell [is] naked before him, and destruction hath no covering.
He stretcheth out the north over the empty place, [and] hangeth the earth upon nothing.
He bindeth up the waters in his thick clouds; and the cloud is not rent under them.
He holdeth back the face of his throne, [and] spreadeth his cloud upon it.
He hath compassed the waters with bounds, until the day and night come to an end.
The pillars of heaven tremble and are astonished at his reproof.
He divideth the sea with his power, and by his understanding he smiteth through the proud.
By his spirit he hath garnished the heavens; his hand hath formed the crooked serpent.
Lo, these [are] parts of his ways: but how little a portion is heard of him? but the thunder of his power who can understand?
Moreover Job continued his parable, and said,
[As] God liveth, [who] hath taken away my judgment; and the Almighty, [who] hath vexed my soul;
All the while my breath [is] in me, and the spirit of God [is] in my nostrils;
My lips shall not speak wickedness, nor my tongue utter deceit.
God forbid that I should justify you: till I die I will not remove mine integrity from me.
My righteousness I hold fast, and will not let it go: my heart shall not reproach [me] so long as I live.
Let mine enemy be as the wicked, and he that riseth up against me as the unrighteous.
For what [is] the hope of the hypocrite, though he hath gained, when God taketh away his soul?
Will God hear his cry when trouble cometh upon him?
Will he delight himself in the Almighty? will he always call upon God?
I will teach you by the hand of God: [that] which [is] with the Almighty will I not conceal.
Behold, all ye yourselves have seen [it;] why then are ye thus altogether vain?
This [is] the portion of a wicked man with God, and the heritage of oppressors, [which] they shall receive of the Almighty.
If his children be multiplied, [it is] for the sword: and his offspring shall not be satisfied with bread.
Those that remain of him shall be buried in death: and his widows shall not weep.
Though he heap up silver as the dust, and prepare raiment as the clay;
He may prepare [it,] but the just shall put [it] on, and the innocent shall divide the silver.
He buildeth his house as a moth, and as a booth [that] the keeper maketh.
The rich man shall lie down, but he shall not be gathered: he openeth his eyes, and he [is] not.
Terrors take hold on him as waters, a tempest stealeth him away in the night.
The east wind carrieth him away, and he departeth: and as a storm hurleth him out of his place.
For [God] shall cast upon him, and not spare: he would fain flee out of his hand.
[Men] shall clap their hands at him, and shall hiss him out of his place.
Surely there is a vein for the silver, and a place for gold [where] they fine [it. ]
Iron is taken out of the earth, and brass [is] molten [out of] the stone.
He setteth an end to darkness, and searcheth out all perfection: the stones of darkness, and the shadow of death.
The flood breaketh out from the inhabitant; [even the waters] forgotten of the foot: they are dried up, they are gone away from men.
[As for] the earth, out of it cometh bread: and under it is turned up as it were fire.
The stones of it [are] the place of sapphires: and it hath dust of gold.
[There is] a path which no fowl knoweth, and which the vulture’s eye hath not seen:
The lion’s whelps have not trodden it, nor the fierce lion passed by it.
He putteth forth his hand upon the rock; he overturneth the mountains by the roots.
He cutteth out rivers among the rocks; and his eye seeth every precious thing.
He bindeth the floods from overflowing; and [the thing that is] hid bringeth he forth to light.
But where shall wisdom be found? and where [is] the place of understanding?
Man knoweth not the price thereof; neither is it found in the land of the living.
The depth saith, It [is] not in me: and the sea saith, [It is] not with me.
It cannot be gotten for gold, neither shall silver be weighed [for] the price thereof.
It cannot be valued with the gold of Ophir, with the precious onyx, or the sapphire.
The gold and the crystal cannot equal it: and the exchange of it [shall not be for] jewels of fine gold.
No mention shall be made of coral, or of pearls: for the price of wisdom [is] above rubies.
The topaz of Ethiopia shall not equal it, neither shall it be valued with pure gold.
Whence then cometh wisdom? and where [is] the place of understanding?
Seeing it is hid from the eyes of all living, and kept close from the fowls of the air.
Destruction and death say, We have heard the fame thereof with our ears.
God understandeth the way thereof, and he knoweth the place thereof.
For he looketh to the ends of the earth, [and] seeth under the whole heaven;
To make the weight for the winds; and he weigheth the waters by measure.
When he made a decree for the rain, and a way for the lightning of the thunder:
Then did he see it, and declare it; he prepared it, yea, and searched it out.
And unto man he said, Behold, the fear of the Lord, that [is] wisdom; and to depart from evil [is] understanding.
Moreover Job continued his parable, and said,
Oh that I were as [in] months past, as [in] the days [when] God preserved me;
When his candle shined upon my head, [and when] by his light I walked [through] darkness;
As I was in the days of my youth, when the secret of God [was] upon my tabernacle;
When the Almighty [was] yet with me, [when] my children [were] about me;
When I washed my steps with butter, and the rock poured me out rivers of oil;
When I went out to the gate through the city, [when] I prepared my seat in the street!
The young men saw me, and hid themselves: and the aged arose, [and] stood up.
The princes refrained talking, and laid [their] hand on their mouth.
The nobles held their peace, and their tongue cleaved to the roof of their mouth.
When the ear heard [me,] then it blessed me; and when the eye saw [me,] it gave witness to me:
Because I delivered the poor that cried, and the fatherless, and [him that had] none to help him.
The blessing of him that was ready to perish came upon me: and I caused the widow’s heart to sing for joy.
I put on righteousness, and it clothed me: my judgment [was] as a robe and a diadem.
I was eyes to the blind, and feet [was] I to the lame.
I [was] a father to the poor: and the cause [which] I knew not I searched out.
And I brake the jaws of the wicked, and plucked the spoil out of his teeth.
Then I said, I shall die in my nest, and I shall multiply [my] days as the sand.
My root [was] spread out by the waters, and the dew lay all night upon my branch.
My glory [was] fresh in me, and my bow was renewed in my hand.
Unto me [men] gave ear, and waited, and kept silence at my counsel.
After my words they spake not again; and my speech dropped upon them.
And they waited for me as for the rain; and they opened their mouth wide [as] for the latter rain.
[If] I laughed on them, they believed [it] not; and the light of my countenance they cast not down.
I chose out their way, and sat chief, and dwelt as a king in the army, as one [that] comforteth the mourners.
But now [they that are] younger than I have me in derision, whose fathers I would have disdained to have set with the dogs of my flock.
Yea, whereto [might] the strength of their hands [profit] me, in whom old age was perished?
For want and famine [they were] solitary; fleeing into the wilderness in former time desolate and waste.
Who cut up mallows by the bushes, and juniper roots [for] their meat.
They were driven forth from among [men,] (they cried after them as [after] a thief;)
To dwell in the cliffs of the valleys, [in] caves of the earth, and [in] the rocks.
Among the bushes they brayed; under the nettles they were gathered together.
[They were] children of fools, yea, children of base men: they were viler than the earth.
And now am I their song, yea, I am their byword.
They abhor me, they flee far from me, and spare not to spit in my face.
Because he hath loosed my cord, and afflicted me, they have also let loose the bridle before me.
Upon [my] right [hand] rise the youth; they push away my feet, and they raise up against me the ways of their destruction.
They mar my path, they set forward my calamity, they have no helper.
They came [upon me] as a wide breaking in [of waters:] in the desolation they rolled themselves [upon me. ]
Terrors are turned upon me: they pursue my soul as the wind: and my welfare passeth away as a cloud.
And now my soul is poured out upon me; the days of affliction have taken hold upon me.
My bones are pierced in me in the night season: and my sinews take no rest.
By the great force [of my disease] is my garment changed: it bindeth me about as the collar of my coat.
He hath cast me into the mire, and I am become like dust and ashes.
I cry unto thee, and thou dost not hear me: I stand up, and thou regardest me [not. ]
Thou art become cruel to me: with thy strong hand thou opposest thyself against me.
Thou liftest me up to the wind; thou causest me to ride [upon it,] and dissolvest my substance.
For I know [that] thou wilt bring me [to] death, and [to] the house appointed for all living.
Howbeit he will not stretch out [his] hand to the grave, though they cry in his destruction.
Did not I weep for him that was in trouble? was [not] my soul grieved for the poor?
When I looked for good, then evil came [unto me:] and when I waited for light, there came darkness.
My bowels boiled, and rested not: the days of affliction prevented me.
I went mourning without the sun: I stood up, [and] I cried in the congregation.
I am a brother to dragons, and a companion to owls.
My skin is black upon me, and my bones are burned with heat.
My harp also is [turned] to mourning, and my organ into the voice of them that weep.
I made a covenant with mine eyes; why then should I think upon a maid?
For what portion of God [is there] from above? and [what] inheritance of the Almighty from on high?
[Is] not destruction to the wicked? and a strange [punishment] to the workers of iniquity?
Doth not he see my ways, and count all my steps?
If I have walked with vanity, or if my foot hath hasted to deceit;
Let me be weighed in an even balance, that God may know mine integrity.
If my step hath turned out of the way, and mine heart walked after mine eyes, and if any blot hath cleaved to mine hands;
[Then] let me sow, and let another eat; yea, let my offspring be rooted out.
If mine heart have been deceived by a woman, or [if] I have laid wait at my neighbour’s door;
[Then] let my wife grind unto another, and let others bow down upon her.
For this [is] an heinous crime; yea, it [is] an iniquity [to be punished by] the judges.
For it [is] a fire [that] consumeth to destruction, and would root out all mine increase.
If I did despise the cause of my manservant or of my maidservant, when they contended with me;
What then shall I do when God riseth up? and when he visiteth, what shall I answer him?
Did not he that made me in the womb make him? and did not one fashion us in the womb?
If I have withheld the poor from [their] desire, or have caused the eyes of the widow to fail;
Or have eaten my morsel myself alone, and the fatherless hath not eaten thereof;
(For from my youth he was brought up with me, as [with] a father, and I have guided her from my mother’s womb;)
If I have seen any perish for want of clothing, or any poor without covering;
If his loins have not blessed me, and [if] he were [not] warmed with the fleece of my sheep;
If I have lifted up my hand against the fatherless, when I saw my help in the gate:
[Then] let mine arm fall from my shoulder blade, and mine arm be broken from the bone.
For destruction [from] God [was] a terror to me, and by reason of his highness I could not endure.
If I have made gold my hope, or have said to the fine gold, [Thou art] my confidence;
If I rejoiced because my wealth [was] great, and because mine hand had gotten much;
If I beheld the sun when it shined, or the moon walking [in] brightness;
And my heart hath been secretly enticed, or my mouth hath kissed my hand:
This also [were] an iniquity [to be punished by] the judge: for I should have denied the God [that is] above.
If I rejoiced at the destruction of him that hated me, or lifted up myself when evil found him:
Neither have I suffered my mouth to sin by wishing a curse to his soul.
If the men of my tabernacle said not, Oh that we had of his flesh! we cannot be satisfied.
The stranger did not lodge in the street: [but] I opened my doors to the traveller.
If I covered my transgressions as Adam, by hiding mine iniquity in my bosom:
Did I fear a great multitude, or did the contempt of families terrify me, that I kept silence, [and] went not out of the door?
Oh that one would hear me! behold, my desire [is, that] the Almighty would answer me, and [that] mine adversary had written a book.
Surely I would take it upon my shoulder, [and] bind it [as] a crown to me.
I would declare unto him the number of my steps; as a prince would I go near unto him.
If my land cry against me, or that the furrows likewise thereof complain;
If I have eaten the fruits thereof without money, or have caused the owners thereof to lose their life:
Let thistles grow instead of wheat, and cockle instead of barley. The words of Job are ended.
So these three men ceased to answer Job, because he [was] righteous in his own eyes.
Then was kindled the wrath of Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the kindred of Ram: against Job was his wrath kindled, because he justified himself rather than God.
Also against his three friends was his wrath kindled, because they had found no answer, and yet had condemned Job.
Now Elihu had waited till Job had spoken, because they were elder than he.
When Elihu saw that [there was] no answer in the mouth of [these] three men, then his wrath was kindled.
And Elihu the son of Barachel the Buzite answered and said, I [am] young, and ye [are] very old; wherefore I was afraid, and durst not shew you mine opinion.
I said, Days should speak, and multitude of years should teach wisdom.
But [there is] a spirit in man: and the inspiration of the Almighty giveth them understanding.
Great men are not [always] wise: neither do the aged understand judgment.
Therefore I said, Hearken to me; I also will shew mine opinion.
Behold, I waited for your words; I gave ear to your reasons, whilst ye searched out what to say.
Yea, I attended unto you, and, behold, [there was] none of you that convinced Job, or that answered his words:
Lest ye should say, We have found out wisdom: God thrusteth him down, not man.
Now he hath not directed [his] words against me: neither will I answer him with your speeches.
They were amazed, they answered no more: they left off speaking.
When I had waited, (for they spake not, but stood still, [and] answered no more;)
[I said,] I will answer also my part, I also will shew mine opinion.
For I am full of matter, the spirit within me constraineth me.
Behold, my belly [is] as wine [which] hath no vent; it is ready to burst like new bottles.
I will speak, that I may be refreshed: I will open my lips and answer.
Let me not, I pray you, accept any man’s person, neither let me give flattering titles unto man.
For I know not to give flattering titles; [in so doing] my maker would soon take me away.
Wherefore, Job, I pray thee, hear my speeches, and hearken to all my words.
Behold, now I have opened my mouth, my tongue hath spoken in my mouth.
My words [shall be of] the uprightness of my heart: and my lips shall utter knowledge clearly.
The Spirit of God hath made me, and the breath of the Almighty hath given me life.
If thou canst answer me, set [thy words] in order before me, stand up.
Behold, I [am] according to thy wish in God’s stead: I also am formed out of the clay.
Behold, my terror shall not make thee afraid, neither shall my hand be heavy upon thee.
Surely thou hast spoken in mine hearing, and I have heard the voice of [thy] words, [saying, ]
I am clean without transgression, I [am] innocent; neither [is there] iniquity in me.
Behold, he findeth occasions against me, he counteth me for his enemy,
He putteth my feet in the stocks, he marketh all my paths.
Behold, [in] this thou art not just: I will answer thee, that God is greater than man.
Why dost thou strive against him? for he giveth not account of any of his matters.
For God speaketh once, yea twice, [yet man] perceiveth it not.
In a dream, in a vision of the night, when deep sleep falleth upon men, in slumberings upon the bed;
Then he openeth the ears of men, and sealeth their instruction,
That he may withdraw man [from his] purpose, and hide pride from man.
He keepeth back his soul from the pit, and his life from perishing by the sword.
He is chastened also with pain upon his bed, and the multitude of his bones with strong [pain: ]
So that his life abhorreth bread, and his soul dainty meat.
His flesh is consumed away, that it cannot be seen; and his bones [that] were not seen stick out.
Yea, his soul draweth near unto the grave, and his life to the destroyers.
If there be a messenger with him, an interpreter, one among a thousand, to shew unto man his uprightness:
Then he is gracious unto him, and saith, Deliver him from going down to the pit: I have found a ransom.
His flesh shall be fresher than a child’s: he shall return to the days of his youth:
He shall pray unto God, and he will be favourable unto him: and he shall see his face with joy: for he will render unto man his righteousness.
He looketh upon men, and [if any] say, I have sinned, and perverted [that which was] right, and it profited me not;
He will deliver his soul from going into the pit, and his life shall see the light.
Lo, all these [things] worketh God oftentimes with man,
To bring back his soul from the pit, to be enlightened with the light of the living.
Mark well, O Job, hearken unto me: hold thy peace, and I will speak.
If thou hast any thing to say, answer me: speak, for I desire to justify thee.
If not, hearken unto me: hold thy peace, and I shall teach thee wisdom.
Furthermore Elihu answered and said,
Hear my words, O ye wise [men;] and give ear unto me, ye that have knowledge.
For the ear trieth words, as the mouth tasteth meat.
Let us choose to us judgment: let us know among ourselves what [is] good.
For Job hath said, I am righteous: and God hath taken away my judgment.
Should I lie against my right? my wound [is] incurable without transgression.
What man [is] like Job, [who] drinketh up scorning like water?
Which goeth in company with the workers of iniquity, and walketh with wicked men.
For he hath said, It profiteth a man nothing that he should delight himself with God.
Therefore hearken unto me, ye men of understanding: far be it from God, [that he should do] wickedness; and [from] the Almighty, [that he should commit] iniquity.
For the work of a man shall he render unto him, and cause every man to find according to [his] ways.
Yea, surely God will not do wickedly, neither will the Almighty pervert judgment.
Who hath given him a charge over the earth? or who hath disposed the whole world?
If he set his heart upon man, [if] he gather unto himself his spirit and his breath;
All flesh shall perish together, and man shall turn again unto dust.
If now [thou hast] understanding, hear this: hearken to the voice of my words.
Shall even he that hateth right govern? and wilt thou condemn him that is most just?
[Is it fit] to say to a king, [Thou art] wicked? [and] to princes, [Ye are] ungodly?
[How much less to him] that accepteth not the persons of princes, nor regardeth the rich more than the poor? for they all [are] the work of his hands.
In a moment shall they die, and the people shall be troubled at midnight, and pass away: and the mighty shall be taken away without hand.
For his eyes [are] upon the ways of man, and he seeth all his goings.
[There is] no darkness, nor shadow of death, where the workers of iniquity may hide themselves.
For he will not lay upon man more [than right;] that he should enter into judgment with God.
He shall break in pieces mighty men without number, and set others in their stead.
Therefore he knoweth their works, and he overturneth [them] in the night, so that they are destroyed.
He striketh them as wicked men in the open sight of others;
Because they turned back from him, and would not consider any of his ways:
So that they cause the cry of the poor to come unto him, and he heareth the cry of the afflicted.
When he giveth quietness, who then can make trouble? and when he hideth [his] face, who then can behold him? whether [it be done] against a nation, or against a man only:
That the hypocrite reign not, lest the people be ensnared.
Surely it is meet to be said unto God, I have borne [chastisement,] I will not offend [any more: ]
[That which] I see not teach thou me: if I have done iniquity, I will do no more.
[Should it be] according to thy mind? he will recompense it, whether thou refuse, or whether thou choose; and not I: therefore speak what thou knowest.
Let men of understanding tell me, and let a wise man hearken unto me.
Job hath spoken without knowledge, and his words [were] without wisdom.
My desire [is that] Job may be tried unto the end because of [his] answers for wicked men.
For he addeth rebellion unto his sin, he clappeth [his hands] among us, and multiplieth his words against God.
Elihu spake moreover, and said,
Thinkest thou this to be right, [that] thou saidst, My righteousness [is] more than God’s?
For thou saidst, What advantage will it be unto thee? [and,] What profit shall I have, [if I be cleansed] from my sin?
I will answer thee, and thy companions with thee.
Look unto the heavens, and see; and behold the clouds [which] are higher than thou.
If thou sinnest, what doest thou against him? or [if] thy transgressions be multiplied, what doest thou unto him?
If thou be righteous, what givest thou him? or what receiveth he of thine hand?
Thy wickedness [may hurt] a man as thou [art;] and thy righteousness [may profit] the son of man.
By reason of the multitude of oppressions they make [the oppressed] to cry: they cry out by reason of the arm of the mighty.
But none saith, Where [is] God my maker, who giveth songs in the night;
Who teacheth us more than the beasts of the earth, and maketh us wiser than the fowls of heaven?
There they cry, but none giveth answer, because of the pride of evil men.
Surely God will not hear vanity, neither will the Almighty regard it.
Although thou sayest thou shalt not see him, [yet] judgment [is] before him; therefore trust thou in him.
But now, because [it is] not [so,] he hath visited in his anger; yet he knoweth [it] not in great extremity:
Therefore doth Job open his mouth in vain; he multiplieth words without knowledge.
Elihu also proceeded, and said,
Suffer me a little, and I will shew thee that [I have] yet to speak on God’s behalf.
I will fetch my knowledge from afar, and will ascribe righteousness to my Maker.
For truly my words [shall] not [be] false: he that is perfect in knowledge [is] with thee.
Behold, God [is] mighty, and despiseth not [any: he is] mighty in strength [and] wisdom.
He preserveth not the life of the wicked: but giveth right to the poor.
He withdraweth not his eyes from the righteous: but with kings [are they] on the throne; yea, he doth establish them for ever, and they are exalted.
And if [they be] bound in fetters, [and] be holden in cords of affliction;
Then he sheweth them their work, and their transgressions that they have exceeded.
He openeth also their ear to discipline, and commandeth that they return from iniquity.
If they obey and serve [him,] they shall spend their days in prosperity, and their years in pleasures.
But if they obey not, they shall perish by the sword, and they shall die without knowledge.
But the hypocrites in heart heap up wrath: they cry not when he bindeth them.
They die in youth, and their life [is] among the unclean.
He delivereth the poor in his affliction, and openeth their ears in oppression.
Even so would he have removed thee out of the strait [into] a broad place, where [there is] no straitness; and that which [should be] set on thy table should be full of fatness.
But thou hast fulfilled the judgment of the wicked: judgment and justice take hold [on thee. ]
Because [there is] wrath, [beware] lest he take thee away with [his] stroke: then a great ransom cannot deliver thee.
Will he esteem thy riches? [no,] not gold, nor all the forces of strength.
Desire not the night, when people are cut off in their place.
Take heed, regard not iniquity: for this hast thou chosen rather than affliction.
Behold, God exalteth by his power: who teacheth like him?
Who hath enjoined him his way? or who can say, Thou hast wrought iniquity?
Remember that thou magnify his work, which men behold.
Every man may see it; man may behold [it] afar off.
Behold, God [is] great, and we know [him] not, neither can the number of his years be searched out.
For he maketh small the drops of water: they pour down rain according to the vapour thereof:
Which the clouds do drop [and] distil upon man abundantly.
Also can [any] understand the spreadings of the clouds, [or] the noise of his tabernacle?
Behold, he spreadeth his light upon it, and covereth the bottom of the sea.
For by them judgeth he the people; he giveth meat in abundance.
With clouds he covereth the light; and commandeth it [not to shine] by [the cloud] that cometh betwixt.
The noise thereof sheweth concerning it, the cattle also concerning the vapour.
At this also my heart trembleth, and is moved out of his place.
Hear attentively the noise of his voice, and the sound [that] goeth out of his mouth.
He directeth it under the whole heaven, and his lightning unto the ends of the earth.
After it a voice roareth: he thundereth with the voice of his excellency; and he will not stay them when his voice is heard.
God thundereth marvellously with his voice; great things doeth he, which we cannot comprehend.
For he saith to the snow, Be thou [on] the earth; likewise to the small rain, and to the great rain of his strength.
He sealeth up the hand of every man; that all men may know his work.
Then the beasts go into dens, and remain in their places.
Out of the south cometh the whirlwind: and cold out of the north.
By the breath of God frost is given: and the breadth of the waters is straitened.
Also by watering he wearieth the thick cloud: he scattereth his bright cloud:
And it is turned round about by his counsels: that they may do whatsoever he commandeth them upon the face of the world in the earth.
He causeth it to come, whether for correction, or for his land, or for mercy.
Hearken unto this, O Job: stand still, and consider the wondrous works of God.
Dost thou know when God disposed them, and caused the light of his cloud to shine?
Dost thou know the balancings of the clouds, the wondrous works of him which is perfect in knowledge?
How thy garments [are] warm, when he quieteth the earth by the south [wind? ]
Hast thou with him spread out the sky, [which is] strong, [and] as a molten looking glass?
Teach us what we shall say unto him; [for] we cannot order [our speech] by reason of darkness.
Shall it be told him that I speak? if a man speak, surely he shall be swallowed up.
And now [men] see not the bright light which is in the clouds: but the wind passeth, and cleanseth them.
Fair weather cometh out of the north: with God is terrible majesty.
[Touching] the Almighty, we cannot find him out: [he is] excellent in power, and in judgment, and in plenty of justice: he will not afflict.
Men do therefore fear him: he respecteth not any [that are] wise of heart.
Then the LORD answered Job out of the whirlwind, and said,
Who [is] this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge?
Gird up now thy loins like a man; for I will demand of thee, and answer thou me.
Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? declare, if thou hast understanding.
Who hath laid the measures thereof, if thou knowest? or who hath stretched the line upon it?
Whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened? or who laid the corner stone thereof;
When the morning stars sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy?
Or [who] shut up the sea with doors, when it brake forth, [as if] it had issued out of the womb?
When I made the cloud the garment thereof, and thick darkness a swaddlingband for it,
And brake up for it my decreed [place,] and set bars and doors,
And said, Hitherto shalt thou come, but no further: and here shall thy proud waves be stayed?
Hast thou commanded the morning since thy days; [and] caused the dayspring to know his place;
That it might take hold of the ends of the earth, that the wicked might be shaken out of it?
It is turned as clay [to] the seal; and they stand as a garment.
And from the wicked their light is withholden, and the high arm shall be broken.
Hast thou entered into the springs of the sea? or hast thou walked in the search of the depth?
Have the gates of death been opened unto thee? or hast thou seen the doors of the shadow of death?
Hast thou perceived the breadth of the earth? declare if thou knowest it all.
Where [is] the way [where] light dwelleth? and [as for] darkness, where [is] the place thereof,
That thou shouldest take it to the bound thereof, and that thou shouldest know the paths [to] the house thereof?
Knowest thou [it,] because thou wast then born? or [because] the number of thy days [is] great?
Hast thou entered into the treasures of the snow? or hast thou seen the treasures of the hail,
Which I have reserved against the time of trouble, against the day of battle and war?
By what way is the light parted, [which] scattereth the east wind upon the earth?
Who hath divided a watercourse for the overflowing of waters, or a way for the lightning of thunder;
To cause it to rain on the earth, [where] no man [is; on] the wilderness, wherein [there is] no man;
To satisfy the desolate and waste [ground;] and to cause the bud of the tender herb to spring forth?
Hath the rain a father? or who hath begotten the drops of dew?
Out of whose womb came the ice? and the hoary frost of heaven, who hath gendered it?
The waters are hid as [with] a stone, and the face of the deep is frozen.
Canst thou bind the sweet influences of Pleiades, or loose the bands of Orion?
Canst thou bring forth Mazzaroth in his season? or canst thou guide Arcturus with his sons?
Knowest thou the ordinances of heaven? canst thou set the dominion thereof in the earth?
Canst thou lift up thy voice to the clouds, that abundance of waters may cover thee?
Canst thou send lightnings, that they may go, and say unto thee, Here we [are? ]
Who hath put wisdom in the inward parts? or who hath given understanding to the heart?
Who can number the clouds in wisdom? or who can stay the bottles of heaven,
When the dust groweth into hardness, and the clods cleave fast together?
Wilt thou hunt the prey for the lion? or fill the appetite of the young lions,
When they couch in [their] dens, [and] abide in the covert to lie in wait?
Who provideth for the raven his food? when his young ones cry unto God, they wander for lack of meat.
Knowest thou the time when the wild goats of the rock bring forth? [or] canst thou mark when the hinds do calve?
Canst thou number the months [that] they fulfil? or knowest thou the time when they bring forth?
They bow themselves, they bring forth their young ones, they cast out their sorrows.
Their young ones are in good liking, they grow up with corn; they go forth, and return not unto them.
Who hath sent out the wild ass free? or who hath loosed the bands of the wild ass?
Whose house I have made the wilderness, and the barren land his dwellings.
He scorneth the multitude of the city, neither regardeth he the crying of the driver.
The range of the mountains [is] his pasture, and he searcheth after every green thing.
Will the unicorn be willing to serve thee, or abide by thy crib?
Canst thou bind the unicorn with his band in the furrow? or will he harrow the valleys after thee?
Wilt thou trust him, because his strength [is] great? or wilt thou leave thy labour to him?
Wilt thou believe him, that he will bring home thy seed, and gather [it into] thy barn?
[Gavest thou] the goodly wings unto the peacocks? or wings and feathers unto the ostrich?
Which leaveth her eggs in the earth, and warmeth them in dust,
And forgetteth that the foot may crush them, or that the wild beast may break them.
She is hardened against her young ones, as though [they were] not hers: her labour is in vain without fear;
Because God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he imparted to her understanding.
What time she lifteth up herself on high, she scorneth the horse and his rider.
Hath thou given the horse strength? hast thou clothed his neck with thunder?
Canst thou make him afraid as a grasshopper? the glory of his nostrils [is] terrible.
He paweth in the valley, and rejoiceth in [his] strength: he goeth on to meet the armed men.
He mocketh at fear, and is not affrighted; neither turneth he back from the sword.
The quiver rattleth against him, the glittering spear and the shield.
He swalloweth the ground with fierceness and rage: neither believeth he that [it is] the sound of the trumpet.
He saith among the trumpets, Ha, ha; and he smelleth the battle afar off, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
Doth the hawk fly by thy wisdom, [and] stretch her wings toward the south?
Doth the eagle mount up at thy command, and make her nest on high?
She dwelleth and abideth on the rock, upon the crag of the rock, and the strong place.
From thence she seeketh the prey, [and] her eyes behold afar off.
Her young ones also suck up blood: and where the slain [are,] there [is] she.
Moreover the LORD answered Job, and said,
Shall he that contendeth with the Almighty instruct [him?] he that reproveth God, let him answer it.
Then Job answered the LORD, and said,
Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth.
Once have I spoken; but I will not answer: yea, twice; but I will proceed no further.
Then answered the LORD unto Job out of the whirlwind, and said,
Gird up thy loins now like a man: I will demand of thee, and declare thou unto me.
Wilt thou also disannul my judgment? wilt thou condemn me, that thou mayest be righteous?
Hast thou an arm like God? or canst thou thunder with a voice like him?
Deck thyself now [with] majesty and excellency; and array thyself with glory and beauty.
Cast abroad the rage of thy wrath: and behold every one [that is] proud, and abase him.
Look on every one [that is] proud, [and] bring him low; and tread down the wicked in their place.
Hide them in the dust together; [and] bind their faces in secret.
Then will I also confess unto thee that thine own right hand can save thee.
Behold now behemoth, which I made with thee; he eateth grass as an ox.
Lo now, his strength [is] in his loins, and his force [is] in the navel of his belly.
He moveth his tail like a cedar: the sinews of his stones are wrapped together.
His bones [are as] strong pieces of brass; his bones [are] like bars of iron.
He [is] the chief of the ways of God: he that made him can make his sword to approach [unto him. ]
Surely the mountains bring him forth food, where all the beasts of the field play.
He lieth under the shady trees, in the covert of the reed, and fens.
The shady trees cover him [with] their shadow; the willows of the brook compass him about.
Behold, he drinketh up a river, [and] hasteth not: he trusteth that he can draw up Jordan into his mouth.
He taketh it with his eyes: [his] nose pierceth through snares.
Canst thou draw out leviathan with an hook? or his tongue with a cord [which] thou lettest down?
Canst thou put an hook into his nose? or bore his jaw through with a thorn?
Will he make many supplications unto thee? will he speak soft [words] unto thee?
Will he make a covenant with thee? wilt thou take him for a servant for ever?
Wilt thou play with him as [with] a bird? or wilt thou bind him for thy maidens?
Shall the companions make a banquet of him? shall they part him among the merchants?
Canst thou fill his skin with barbed irons? or his head with fish spears?
Lay thine hand upon him, remember the battle, do no more.
Behold, the hope of him is in vain: shall not [one] be cast down even at the sight of him?
None [is so] fierce that dare stir him up: who then is able to stand before me?
Who hath prevented me, that I should repay [him? whatsoever is] under the whole heaven is mine.
I will not conceal his parts, nor his power, nor his comely proportion.
Who can discover the face of his garment? [or] who can come [to him] with his double bridle?
Who can open the doors of his face? his teeth [are] terrible round about.
[His] scales [are his] pride, shut up together [as with] a close seal.
One is so near to another, that no air can come between them.
They are joined one to another, they stick together, that they cannot be sundered.
By his neesings a light doth shine, and his eyes [are] like the eyelids of the morning.
Out of his mouth go burning lamps, [and] sparks of fire leap out.
Out of his nostrils goeth smoke, as [out] of a seething pot or caldron.
His breath kindleth coals, and a flame goeth out of his mouth.
In his neck remaineth strength, and sorrow is turned into joy before him.
The flakes of his flesh are joined together: they are firm in themselves; they cannot be moved.
His heart is as firm as a stone; yea, as hard as a piece of the nether [millstone. ]
When he raiseth up himself, the mighty are afraid: by reason of breakings they purify themselves.
The sword of him that layeth at him cannot hold: the spear, the dart, nor the habergeon.
He esteemeth iron as straw, [and] brass as rotten wood.
The arrow cannot make him flee: slingstones are turned with him into stubble.
Darts are counted as stubble: he laugheth at the shaking of a spear.
Sharp stones [are] under him: he spreadeth sharp pointed things upon the mire.
He maketh the deep to boil like a pot: he maketh the sea like a pot of ointment.
He maketh a path to shine after him; [one] would think the deep [to be] hoary.
Upon earth there is not his like, who is made without fear.
He beholdeth all high [things:] he [is] a king over all the children of pride.
Then Job answered the LORD, and said,
I know that thou canst do every [thing,] and [that] no thought can be withholden from thee.
Who [is] he that hideth counsel without knowledge? therefore have I uttered that I understood not; things too wonderful for me, which I knew not.
Hear, I beseech thee, and I will speak: I will demand of thee, and declare thou unto me.
I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee.
Wherefore I abhor [myself,] and repent in dust and ashes.

And it was [so,] that after the LORD had spoken these words unto Job, the LORD said to Eliphaz the Temanite, My wrath is kindled against thee, and against thy two friends: for ye have not spoken of me [the thing that is] right, as my servant Job [hath. ]

Therefore take unto you now seven bullocks and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering; and my servant Job shall pray for you: for him will I accept: lest I deal with you [after your] folly, in that ye have not spoken of me [the thing which is] right, like my servant Job.
So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite [and] Zophar the Naamathite went, and did according as the LORD commanded them: the LORD also accepted Job.
And the LORD turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed for his friends: also the LORD gave Job twice as much as he had before.

Then came there unto him all his brethren, and all his sisters, and all they that had been of his acquaintance before, and did eat bread with him in his house: and they bemoaned him, and comforted him over all the evil that the LORD had brought upon him: every man also gave him a piece of money, and every one an earring of gold.
So the LORD blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning: for he had fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she asses.
He had also seven sons and three daughters.
And he called the name of the first, Jemima; and the name of the second, Kezia; and the name of the third, Keren-happuch.
And in all the land were no women found [so] fair as the daughters of Job: and their father gave them inheritance among their brethren.
After this lived Job an hundred and forty years, and saw his sons, and his sons’ sons, [even] four generations.
So Job died, [being] old and full of days.

textCards.body: 「仇敌2!N/oyav’」（24节）原文与「约伯 2iN/eyove’」读音相近、字义相 【伯十三 24】「祢为何掩面、拿我当仇敌呢？ 」 【伯十三 25】「祢要惊动被风吹的叶子吗？要追赶枯干的碎秸吗？ 」 【伯十三 26】「祢按罪状刑罚我，又使我担当幼年的罪孽； 」 【伯十三 27】「也把我的脚上了木狗，并窥察我一切的道路，为我的脚掌划定界限。 」 【伯十三 28】「我已经像灭绝的烂物，像虫蛀的衣裳。 」关。

「仇敌2!N/oyav’」（24节）原文与「约伯 2iN/eyove’」读音相近、字义相 【伯十三 24】「祢为何掩面、拿我当仇敌呢？ 」 【伯十三 25】「祢要惊动被风吹的叶子吗？要追赶枯干的碎秸吗？ 」 【伯十三 26】「祢按罪状刑罚我，又使我担当幼年的罪孽； 」 【伯十三 27】「也把我的脚上了木狗，并窥察我一切的道路，为我的脚掌划定界限。 」 【伯十三 28】「我已经像灭绝的烂物，像虫蛀的衣裳。 」关。

textCards.body: 「君王TVa?/na:ghed’」（37节）原文是「领袖、王子」，与「述说 Tag/na-gad'」（37节）谐音。主前 1250 年的古埃及纸莎草纸《死者之书 book of the dead》 （或译为亡灵书、死亡之书）的一部分。 《死者之书》是图文并茂的卷轴，放在棺材中，内容因人而异。目的都是教导死者在前往阴间的路上保护自己，并回答 42 位神明的审问，以证明自己无罪，甚至不惜用咒语愚弄神明。

textCards.body: 乌斯地，此地的东部边界不详，可能北邻亚兰（创10:22-23），即亚伯拉罕的侄儿及其家人所住的地方（创22:21），也可能靠近西珥的一个后裔，他和以扫的众子比邻而居：以扫众子所住之地也称为以东（创36:28）。本书一开头就宣告约伯很敬虔（并在1:8和2:3再次肯定）：他完全正直（创6:9论到挪亚和创17:1论到亚伯拉罕时，也是用"完全"这个词），并且敬畏上帝，远离恶事（这符合《箴言》中"智慧人"的特征：见箴3:7.14:16.16:6）。这个描述表明，读者所认识的约伯，与后来三个朋友所指控的约伯，有着明显的差别。

textCards.body: 心中弃掉上帝，"弃掉"希伯来原文直译：称颂，然而根据上下文，这里是在表达相反的意思，即：诅咒（见《和修》注；1:11，2:5，9中的"弃掉"和王上21:10，13中的"谤渎"，在原文中也是用同一个动词的推断含义）。《和》用"弃掉"无疑是个委婉的说法，即用推断而非明言某人"诅咒上帝"。引子的描述和对话以特别的方式使用"称颂"一词，与结尾部分（42:7-17）的结论形成辛辣的讽刺。在结尾部分，上帝指示约伯的三个朋友，他们需要约伯的代祷，因为他们对上帝的议论是愚妄的（见42:7-8）。

textCards.body: 上帝的众子（《和修》"上帝的众使者"，指侍立在上帝面前的天上的使者，就像侍立在国王面前的臣宰一样（见15:8：诗29:1：赛6：-8）。希伯来文惯用语中的"的众子'，可指由一个人领导的一群人，那个带领的人被称作他们的*父亲"（如撒上10:12中那群先知的"父亲。撒但，希伯来文名词satan一般用来指敌人（如撒上29:4；王上11:14），但这里指特定的一位（"那控告者"或"那敌人"，它不属于众子的一员，但是也来在其中。下文的对话显示，这位撒但的特性与创3章所描述的蛇一样，而"撒但"这名就是那蛇的专称（如代上21:1：另见启12:9）。

textCards.body: 空虚的人却毫无知识，人生在世好像野驴的驹子（《和修》"空虚的人若获得知识，野驴生下的驹子也成了人2，这则谚语的第一句是否像第二句一样，在表达一种不可能，正如野驴不可能生出人或家养的驹子？两句之间的关系不是很清楚，可能是一种平行句法，然而琐法援引这则谚语的用意十分清楚。他劝约伯不要再愚不可及，因为愚拙就像空虚的人的道路，永远不会通向知识。琐法劝约伯不要再坚持自己是义人，要回转、祷告和悔改，寻求上帝（13-20节）。

textCards.body: 琐法对恶人命运作的最后陈辞，与他在13-19节的描述形成鲜明对比：琐法在那几节经文中说，只要约伯悔改，就必得到上帝赐福。琐法的陈述是为了警告约伯不要再走现在的道路（见18节，以及与指望的对比）。

textCards.body: 本节蕴含的讽刺要等到最后结尾才向琐法显明（见42:7-9）。虽然琐法以责备的口吻质问约伯，他是否能够测透上帝高深的作为，然而他自己却早已假定上帝叫约伯受苦的目的，显而易见就是为了责备约伯并呼吁他悔改。

textCards.body: 他放松君王的绑（《和修》"他解除君王的权势"，即君王失去王位。"绑"可以理解为王家的饰带、肩带或腰带，被平民百姓用的腰带所取代。

textCards.body: 他使邦国开广，这句话可能有"分散"或"驱散"的否定意义，从而使第二句与第一句形成对偶。有些时候，上帝使邦国兴起，然后又将其摧毁；有些时候，他先使国家四分五裂，然后再赐下太平，或者引导这国变得秩序井然。

textCards.body: 谁不知道是耶和华的手作成的呢？这一行诗是《约伯记》中，在引子和38-42章以外，唯一出现上帝名字（希伯来原文YHWH）的地方。在希伯来原文中，它与赛41:20中的一行诗句相同。

textCards.body: 他的尊荣，岂不叫你们惧怕么？约伯问他的朋友，他们这样轻率地代表上帝说话，到底有没有认真地对待上帝的荣耀和大能。在后文的回应中，约伯坦陈，每当他想到面对上帝的威荣时，都感到惧怕（31:23）。

textCards.body: 约伯意识到自己在12:17-25所论述的那一位的力量，但仍然禁不住要冒上帝杀他的险。我虽无指望（原文直译：我仍然指望他），指望"是指等待某事；在等待期间，可能存着或失去忍耐，也可能仍持守或已放弃积极的盼望。约伯没有忍耐，并且盼望自己的生命尽快结束。约伯不要等待，他愿意冒死向上帝辩明他的案件（但参14:19）。

textCards.body: 为我的脚掌划定界限，这个比喻指限制行动（"划定界限"，或指跟踪（"打上印记"。这两种意思都有：约伯的脚上了木狗，并且不论他走到哪里，上帝都在监察他。这两件事虽然不能同时发生，但二者都表明上帝时刻严密地审视约伯。

textCards.body: 你们是编造谎言的，"编造"的比喻本身不一定是负面的，其原意是用白色涂料粉饰，指修补出现裂纹或碎裂的东西（如瓦罐），用一种材料粘合碎片并封住裂纹。约伯想要争辩的是，他的朋友试图用来"粉饰"现状的东西，不论是关于约伯还是上帝的，他们都应该知道那些不是事实。

textCards.body: 竞在何处呢？7-14节的重点是生命必定死亡，所以约伯的问题不是想要说明他对死后生命的看法（可能在12节隐约提到），而是指他在受苦和死亡之后，没有机会通过在世上复活来为自己洗雪冤屈。

textCards.body: 谁能使洁净之物出于污秽之中呢？约伯形容人的生命又苦又短，在7:1-10，约伯已经向朋友陈述这个主题；现在他又用这个问题及其答案来指出，没有人能够超出上帝设定的界限（另见14:5）。

textCards.body: 智慧人岂可用虚空的知识回答……呢？可能以利法在问：作为智慧人，他是否应该回应约伯：但是，3-6节的内容显示，这个问题旨在反驳约伯自称聪明（见12:3.13:2）。以利法辩称，约伯的话显明他是一个空话连篇的人，而不是充满智慧。

textCards.body: 敬畏的意，这里虽然没有提到"上帝"一词，但本节的后半部分提到上帝，并且2-6节的猛烈攻击表明这正是以利法的推论：约伯向上帝的抱怨开始变得轻率.并废弃了那能使他得解脱的事（即在上帝面前悔改和谦卑），因而阻止了上帝聆听他的敬虔的心（《和修》"默想"）。

textCards.body: 你曾听见上帝的密旨吗？读者因为已经知道引子中天上的对话（1:7-12，2:2-6），所以一定会觉得这个问题很有讽刺意味。以利法责备约伯的，正是他假定约伯所犯的：他错误地作出结论说，约伯受苦清楚显示他是被上帝审判的。

textCards.body: 缝麻布在我皮肤上，约伯持续的悲痛就像缝在他皮肤上的粗布，不但极其痛苦，而且没有尽头。把我的角放在尘土中，动物的角象征它的力量、能力和尊贵。所有象征约伯的尊贵和价值的外观，都被拿走了。

textCards.body: 约伯有在上……的中保为他作见证，那位是谁呢？是被以利法嘲笑的"诸圣者"中的一位（5:1）吗？约伯曾经尝试恳求一位"听讼的人"（9:33），现在他更加确信。唯独上帝是他受苦的源头。同时，他也进一步认识到，唯独上帝才是他洗雪冤屈的指望。虽然约伯目前可能还不清楚这一点（因此16:21将"见证"人与上帝区别出来），但他最后会明白的：参19:25。

textCards.body: 因为1-4节应该是向上帝说话（3-4节直接向上帝说话），所以在第5节，约伯可能是求上帝纪念他朋友的行为，或者警告朋友这些行为的后果，或是两者都有。许多解经家认为第5节引用了当时的一句谚语，照此理解，当中的信息就类似《旧约》其他经文对作假见证者的警告（见申19:18-19；箴19:5,9）。

约伯记 19:2 研修本注释
要到几时呢？约伯一开口，就回应比勒达在两次发言的开头提出的问题（见8:2.18:2），为要让他的朋友察觉到，他们的谴责是何等无情。

textCards.body: 只剩牙皮，希伯来原文所指的对象不是很清楚（很难确定牙的"皮"是什么意思），按照这里的上下文，一般认为是指勉强达成或仅仅免于某事（正如英语惯用语bytheskinofmyteeth所表达的）：约伯承受着情感和肉体的双重苦楚，他见证自己仅仅逃脱死亡。

textCards.title: 约伯记 19:20 研修本注释

textCards.body: 你们为什么…吃我的肉还以为不足呢？如果约伯的朋友如此确信约伯犯了罪，他身体上的明显苦痛代表着上帝的审判，那么约伯质问他们，为什么还要继续无情地逼迫他呢？从约伯的立场来看，他的朋友是在利用他仅剩的一切来折磨他（见2节），因此他恳请他们怜悯他（见19:23-24约伯希望他的言语能够被记录下来，好在他死后留作见证。他提到两种古代近东常用的记载方法。记录在书上，就是写在书卷上或者刻在泥版上，二者都是比较便于携带的书写材料。用铅灌在磐石上，约伯还希望他的言语能够以比较公开和长久的方式保存下来。这类纪念碑的一个现存例子，是在贝希斯敦（今比索通，位于伊朗西部）的一个悬崖上发现的大流士一世铭文，石碑上的楔形文字灌上了铅，以便下方路上的行人看清铭文的内容。虽然经文没有明确说明，然而这卷书的成文在许多方面实现了他的愿望，包含并超出了约伯的目的。

textCards.title: 约伯记 19:22 研修本注释

textCards.body: 忿怒惹动刀剑的刑罚，朋友的忿怒是"刀剑的罪行"一种当受刑罚的罪（参31:11，28）。作假见证者要受到与被告相同的刑罚（申19:16-19）：朋友的控告若成立，那约伯就该受死刑。知道有报应，"报应"的希伯来原文作"审判"（见《和》注），这句希伯来文的意思隐晦不明，可能指有一位审判者，或者将会有审判。约伯想要表达的意思是：对他的朋友来说，救赎主的出现将是一个坏消息。

textCards.body: 约伯用十次这个数字来象征最大程度，而不是真正发生了十次（另见创31:7，41；民14:22）。

textCards.body: 这里译作倾覆我的动词，与比勒达在8:3所用的动词（译作"偏离"相同。约伯使用这个动词来澄清他的观点：即使他在为自己无罪辩护，他仍然坚信上帝是公正的：他继续申明，他受苦不是因为自己的罪，而是由于上帝的最终许可或上帝有意这样做。

textCards.body: 上帝用篱笆拦住我的道路，上帝的篱笆最初保护约伯远离灾祸（1:10），现在却成为禁锢约伯，使约伯无路可逃的高墙（参3:23）。对约伯来说，从他受苦的极端规模来判断，这必定是来自上帝。

textCards.body: 约伯用适度的责备回应妻子：他并不以为自己完全洞悉她的心意，但约伯警告她不要像愚顽的妇人一样说话。

textCards.body: 从《旧约》可以知道，约伯的三个朋友都是来自南方。以利法来自以东重镇提幔（创36:11.15：结25:13：摩1:11-12），该地以智慧而闻名（耶49:7）。比勒达来自书亚，是亚伯拉罕与基土拉所生之子的名字，书亚的兄弟是米甸，侄儿是示巴和底但（创25:2：代上1:32），底但是以东或阿拉伯的一个地名。琐法来自拿玛，这是该隐家谱中一位妇女的名字（创4:22），基尼人就是该隐的后裔（创4:22）。基尼人还与西奈和阿拉伯沙漠中的米甸人一起被提及（民10:29：士4:11）。安慰是本书的钥词，有关讨论见"导论：文学特征"。

textCards.body: 约伯的朋友很可能会认不出他来，因为约伯除了全身长满毒疮之外，还因悲痛而情绪消沉，外貌也发生了变化（见1:20,2:7-8）。

textCards.body: 沉默持续了七天七夜，表示他们为约伯遭受苦难而哀痛了一个完整的时间。以西结来到那些被掳到巴比伦的人中间，也有类似的反应（见结3:15）。

textCards.summary: 上帝向撒但指出，尽管约伯遇到这么多灾祸，但他仍然持守他的纯正。这是指约伯忠诚的回应，包含了1:20-21描述的悲痛、敬拜和认信。
textCards.title: 约伯记 2:3 研修本注释

textCards.body: 只要存留他的性命，存留约伯的性命不是一种怜悯，也不仅仅是考验必需的让步，而是考验不可或缺的条件。有些时候，生命中最难以承受的悲痛是想死却死不成（参3:20-23,6:9），这才是信心的终极考验。

textCards.body: 虽然作者对约伯妻子的描述很少，但她的话非常值得关注，不仅因为她所说的话正好与天上的对话内容有关，也因为这表明了她的评论的性质。她反问约伯，质疑他所持守的是否明智（你仍然持守你的纯正吗？见3节），尽管正是这些使约伯得蒙上帝的称赞。她认为约伯应该采取的行动是弃掉上帝，死了吧（见1:11，2:4），这也是撒但一直竭力挑唆约伯作出的回应。

textCards.body: 天 地 ，尽管这不是琐法的目的，但他无意中提到了本书主要的张力：在上帝面前的真相与在地上发生的事情之间，到底是怎样的关系？朋友错误地认定，约伯在地上的境遇是他在天上的上帝面前有罪的明显凭据。约伯的生活信念是：上帝确实是公正的。约伯的哀叹反映出他的盼望，即上帝公义的彰显会超过在他现世生命中的彰显。最后，琐法会意识到天要显明的是他的错误，而不是约伯的罪孽（见42:7-9）。

textCards.body: 在之前的发言中，以利法曾经质问约伯，就连天上的众圣者也受上帝审判，他岂敢自称在上帝面前是义人（见4:18.15:15）。约伯在这里重述以利法所声称的真相，为要质疑朋友的观点：他们假定地上发生的事情清楚表明了那是审判，但这与经验相矛盾，因这等于暗示上帝竟然需要提醒，或者需要他们的知识来教导。既然上帝可以审判那些在高位的（原文直译：在高处的），甚或人看不见的，那么约伯的朋友必须格外谨慎，不应单凭在地上所看见的现象，就假定能够彻底洞察上帝的心意。

textCards.body: 在他以后去的更多，除了讨论人的境遇并不一定清楚显示祝福或审判之外，约伯的话还藏着另一个警告：许多人被恶人的外在景况迷惑（恶人是讨论的核心，见30节），以致他们在他活着的时候跟随他，在他死的时候尊荣他。
textCards.searchText:

textCards.title: 约伯记 21:33 研修本注释

textCards.body: 由于约伯始终坚称，恶人在地上兴盛，并且他自己的悲惨境遇不是由于罪，因此以利法认为，约伯暗示上帝的住处过高，以致不能"知道"或审判地上的生活，所以他推诿责任。以利法在此用约伯的问题提出问题（上帝知道什么？），好像诗73:11中恶人所说的话，但诗人其实是对恶人享受兴盛和平安发出哀怨，类似约伯的抱怨（见诗73:1-17）。以利法错误地将约伯对上帝的态度，等同于恶人对上帝的态度。
textCards.searchText:

textCards.body: 以利法恳请约伯"要认识上帝"，暗示他认定自己对约伯境遇的解释与上帝的解释一致。特别是在17-18节的论证，以利法认为他有足够的理由说服约伯，让他心平气和，并最终承认他受苦的根源是在于自己的罪恶。

textCards.body: 这节经文是朋友无意中说出的又一个反讽。以利法暗示，约伯悔改会使他成为中保，为别人代求，甚至为非无辜的人带来拯救。以利法却料不到，是他自己需要他所说的拯救，并且实际上将由约伯为他代求（见42:7-9）。

textCards.body: 约伯回应的第一句话，很可能指向三个朋友多于指向上帝。他开头说"如今……"，暗示经过如此长篇大论的对话之后，朋友既不愿认真聆听他的话，也不能说服他自承有罪。我的责罚 …重（原文直译：我的手是重的），虽然这不是一般的表述方式，然而手"坚壮"（如：赛35:3）或"发软"（如：耶6:24）的比喻，在《旧约》中分别用来指人感到鼓舞或沮丧。
textCards.searchText:
约伯记 23:2 研修本注释

约伯回应的第一句话，很可能指向三个朋友多于指向上帝。他开头说"如今……"，暗示经过如此长篇大论的对话之后，朋友既不愿认真聆听他的话，也不能说服他自承有罪。我的责罚 …重（原文直译：我的手是重的），虽然这不是一般的表述方式，然而手"坚壮"（如：赛35:3）或"发软"（如：耶6:24）的比喻，在《旧约》中分别用来指人感到鼓舞或沮丧。

textCards.title: 约伯记 23:2 研修本注释

textCards.body: 约伯提出的问题有两个角度：为何恶人似乎并未经历罚恶，并且义人似乎从未看见那日子呢？在《旧约》中，"耶和华的日子"是一句常用语，指上帝将要审判万国（见珥21：参摩5:18-20注），也指上帝的荣耀要完全彰显，他百姓的荣美要得着复兴。"耶和华的日子"是全能的上帝显明他作为的时候。

textCards.body: "这些恶人犹如浮萍快快飘去"，可能是本书写作时候的一句谚语，但这句话的所指对象和含义并不明确。"快快"可能指恶人的生命短暂，正如朋友所描述的或者约伯所祈愿的（见24:18-20注）那样。

textCards.title: 约伯记 24:18 研修本注释

textCards.body: 约伯关怀不怀孕、不生养的妇人和寡妇，重述朋友所作指控中的真相（见22:9）。照顾妇、孤儿和寄居者，是律法的一个中心主题（见出22:21-27；申24:17-22）。一方面，这种照顾是上帝百姓敬度的一个重要表现：另一方面，如果在这些事上出了不公义的情况而又没有处理，就表明以色列人已经忘记了自己的历史。虽然在这些事上乘持公义是上帝的要求，但是这些明显存在的不公义，似乎没有任何迹象因受到审判而被遏止。如果约伯的朋友所言属实，那么那些剥削寡妇牟利的人，早就应该受到明显的审判了。

textCards.body: 有些解经家认为，本节第二句"强取穷人的衣服为当头，应该理解为，"强取穷人的孩童为当头"，因为这与第一句强调孤儿更加一致。但在希伯来语法上，孩童并非唯一的指称对象。然而，第3节和10-12节似乎暗示，强取的抵押品可能是穷人赖以温饱的必需品（当然，其影响等同于从喂奶的母亲怀中夺走孩子）。

textCards.body: 在上帝面前人怎能称义？在约伯与朋友的整个对话期间，这个问题以稍微不同的形式重复出现了好几次：由以利法最早提出（4:17），约伯在第二次发言时改述（9:2），以利法重复，并加重语气（15:14），现在比勒达又在朋友的最后发言中再次提出。

textCards.body: 在希伯来原文中，"宝座"与"满月"（参诗81:3）的发音和拼写相同，但是含义不同。如果作者在这里是指"宝座〞，或是刻意让语意模糊，那么这幅景象可能指诸天将上帝隐蔽在他施行统管之处。

textCards.body: 约伯冗长回应的第一个部分，开头是典型的对话形式："约伯回答说"（见26:1.以及自4:1起各次回应的首节）。这个开头和29:1（约伯接着说），将26-31章连成一个整体，作为约伯的最后一次发言，既是最后陈述，也是直接回应他的朋友。

textCards.body: 上帝的作为（《和修》"上帝手所做的"），"手"（希伯来原文yad）常喻指能力，但约伯和朋友关注的问题是公义。以利法声称自己是在代表上帝责备约伯（参22:26-27）。约伯反过来宣告他从上帝领受了启示，是他不能违背的（6:10）：他将不隐瞒上帝所行的（27:11），即全能者的意念。

textCards.body: 译作"上帝为"的希伯来文短语，很多解经家认为，更好的解释是"从上帝"'，即"恶人从上帝所得的份"，这样就与本节的第二句平行。这两种解释的差别只有一个希伯来文字母的不同，可能是因为前面的词而不小心加到这一句上的。然而，因为第11节在希伯来原文中也有类似的用法，所以在这里的上下文中，第13节的意思并不需要改变。这两个短语也是约伯的要点：如果他的纯正在上帝眼中看为真实，那么朋友就应该思想，他们所描述的审判是否会落到他们自己头上。

textCards.body: 这里提到灭没和死亡（"灭没"原文音译"亚巴顿"是语带双关，作者让读者联想起约伯之前也用过类似的表达，描述人类不知道的一个领域（见26:5-6）。这两个名称可能只是简单的拟人化，或者借用异教神话中的观念，如：死亡（希伯来原文mawet）对应迦南神明莫特，指出这些权势也找不到智慧。

textCards.body: 在与朋友的整个对话期间，约伯在多个意象中使用了幽暗一词（参3:5,12:22,16:16,24:17和10:22），当中肯定人的勤奋获得了成功。本章后文清楚表明，约伯使用这些描述来质疑——如果不是暗中责备—一三位朋友，因为他们假定在探索世界的智慧上，或是在分辨别人心中是否有智慧上，都已获得类似的成功。

textCards.body: 学者对古代近东采矿技术的发现和研究，还不足以精确地证实这节经文所描绘的图景。译作"矿穴"的希伯来词，通常用来指间歇河的冲沟（同样，第10节译作"水道"的字，通常用来指"河流"或者专指"尼罗河）。考古学家已经发现了水平矿井，其中一些还与垂直矿井交叉，可能是用于采矿通风的。不论悬在空中是否指这些垂直矿井，本节所绘图景的目的都显而易见：三次描述矿井地处偏僻（无人居住之处、过路的人也想不到和与人远离）.进一步指出人类寻找宝物的困难和努力。

textCards.body: 撒但在指控时辩称，约伯之所以正直，只是因为上帝在他周围圈上祝福的"笆"（1:10）。约伯在对话开场的哀歌中，称他在令人费解的受苦境遇中性命得以存留，好比是被上帝四面围困的人。约伯始终如一的忠信，驳倒了撒但的争辩。约伯对他境遇的哀叹既被上帝责备（见38-41章），又蒙上帝称许（42:7）。

textCards.body: 《圣经》有时以隐喻的方式提到古代神话中的一些内容，并经常藉此形象化地描绘上帝的大能或权柄（参26:12）。约伯提到那些有能力召唤鳄鱼（《和修》"力威亚探"：见诗74:14注）：并且能对某个日子作出咒诅的人，愿他们发出咒语。这是他再次哀叹自己的生日。

textCards.body: 约伯感觉他现在呼求上帝的帮助（另见28节）并没有得到回应，这与约伯以前拯救和帮助那些困苦穷乏的人（见29:12），形成鲜明的对比。30:24-31.约伯在结束这段讲话时，将自己描绘成一个以前他会回应的求救者（24-25节）。然而当他自己身陷困苦的时候，他盼望得好处，灾祸却临到（26节）：他求救（28节），却只得到孤立和悲泣（27、29-31节）。

textCards.body: 这里提到的植物都是人在别无选择的情况下才吃的食物。咸草是一种低矮的茂密灌木，叶子厚而味酸：罗腾是一种矮树，枝干长而直，叶小，并且根有毒。罗腾树的根有毒，燃烧时会发出许多热量（见诗120:4），在希伯来文的拼写和发音上，这个词与另一个词很相似，意思可以是"为他们的温暖"。然而，这里可能是用众所周知的罗腾树根的毒性，来强调这些人极度悲慘的处境。

textCards.body: 约伯声称自己品行端正，回想起自己曾经立志看什么和不看什么，他称之为与眼睛立约。他特别表明在躲避眼目的情欲上；自己是清洁的：怎能恋恋瞻望处女呢？基督信徒应该能够看出这个承诺是明智的，耶稣也有同样的教导（太5:28）。

textCards.body: 作者在对以利户的介绍中提到了他的父亲和家族，比对任何一位朋友的介绍都要详细（见2:11，4:1,8:1，11:1）。这可能表明以利户清楚自己的角色（如：巴拉迦可能指"愿上帝赐福"或者"上帝已赐福》，也可能因为他很年轻而需要多作一些介绍。

textCards.body: 虽然以利户向约伯和他的朋友发怒（2-3节），但本书结尾在使用相同的措辞描述上帝时，上帝的怒气只是针对约伯的三个朋友，因为他们关于上帝的议论，不如约伯所说的那样正确（见42:7）。

textCards.title: 约伯记 33:14 研修本注释

textCards.body: 以利户反复陈明，上帝向人说话的方式是为了防止人的灵魂陷于坑里（另见22、24、28、30节）。因此他暗示，约伯受苦可能是上帝在纠正他人生道路的方向，而不只是因一些隐而未现的罪受到惩罚。然而，考虑到上帝在引子中对约伯的描述（见1:8，2:3），以利户的暗示似乎与三个朋友的指控十分类似，甚至可能更加严厉。

textCards.title: 约伯记 33:18 研修本注释

textCards.body: 以利户在总结约伯的发言时，听来仿佛约伯辩称自已是清洁和无过一样。然而，从约伯定期献.祭可以清楚地看出，这并不是他的宣称（见1:5）。约伯宣称的主旨是，他否认朋友暗示他受苦的根源是有隐藏的罪。以利户错误地概括约伯的辩解，结果提出类似于三个朋友的论证：上帝比世人伟大（33:12），因此他必定是有意警告或责备约伯（14-30节）。

textCards.body: 不必使人到他面前再三鉴察，在希伯来经文中，这句的主语是"他"，具体所指必须根据上下文来推断。有些解经家认为这里是指"人"，意即人并不设定自己受审判的时间，但这样解释需要稍微改动希伯来文本。然而，根据以利户所作争论的上下文，主语为"上帝"是合理的：约伯一直请求能有机会把自己的案件陈明在上帝或者伸裁者面前，但是以利户指出，上帝已经在行动，不需要再多考虑约伯或者其他任何人的案件。

textCards.body: 上膛尝食物，真理通过聆听来分辨，就像食物的品质通过味觉来辨别一样。之前，约伯也用这句谚语来挑战朋友的智慧（12:11）。以利户重复这句谚语，邀请听众来评论约伯的讲话。

textCards.body: 以利户说约伯与作孽的结伴，和恶人同行，这是智慧人需要避免的道路（见诗1:1）。他这样描述约伯，是根据约伯声称自己是公义的，并且是上帝夺去他的理（34:5）：但是上帝必不作恶，他必按人所作的报应人（见11-13节），因此约伯的说法必然显示他自己是恶人。

textCards.body: 虽然约伯曾经说过，恶人和义人似乎有着相同的命运，但他是为要反驳朋友认为恶人必定受刑罚的见解，他并没有说过像以利户在这里所说的话。约伯过的生活是以上帝为乐，并顺服他的话（见23:10-12）；他曾经争辩说.恶人常常兴旺发达，就好像侍奉全能者总是无益一样（见21:15）。

textCards.body: 耶和华从旋风中回答约伯，讲话的首句非常简短，但根据整卷书的脉络背景，当中的象征意义非常重要。三个朋友和以利户都以某种方式，认定约伯的境遇和他的回应，都表明他已弃绝自称一直敬虔地侍奉的上帝。他们替告约伯说，如果他不悔改，承认自己所受的苦难是上帝的责罚，那么他必将受到更大的审判。然而，这段讲话的首句表明，上帝在向约伯显现时，彰显出他荣耀的大能，以及他与约伯的联系。上帝在引子中描述约伯与他的关系时（见1:8.2:3），使用了"耶和华"（希伯来原文YHWH）这个名字，是最常用来说明上帝与人立约的属性和应许的（见出3:14-15）。上帝"回答约伯"这一事实，也与三个朋友和以利户对约伯未来的设想大相径庭（见35:9-13）。虽然以利户曾经描述上帝在自然界的大能和心意（见36:22-37:13），但当耶和华"从旋风中"与约伯说话时，他彰显出自己的大能和临在，这是立约的场景。他来不是简单地称约伯光义，上帝的临在表明，他责备约伯是出于对他坚定的爱，而不是像朋友所说的，是因为约伯离弃义路而受到审判。

textCards.body: 以利户曾经指控约伯说话"没有知识"（34:35，35:16）或没有智慧（34:35），除了有罪之外，还悖逆上帝（34:37）。当耶和华指出，约伯用无知的言语使我的旨意暗昧不明的时候，却没有如此严厉地责备约伯。这里的黑暗和隐藏之事两个概念似乎是一语双关（见约伯在42:3所描绘的意象）。约伯曾经根据他从自己和别人的境遇中得到的启示，对上帝的治理作出结论。然而，他没有充分说明那些向他隐藏的事情，因此他的话使上帝治理的智慧和公义显得晦暗不明。上帝在说话时查问约伯，为要提醒他，即使上帝已对自然界和其中的居民展现出威严的统管和大能，但其中仍有很多事情是向人隐藏的。如果对于创造和受造物尚且如此，那么对于创造主的智慧和心意，又有多少是人能够知道的呢？

textCards.body: 十二宫，在希伯来原文中，这个词没有出现在其他地方：根据上下文，它必定是指一个星座。北斗也是一个星座因为在9:9，它与参星和昴星一起被提及（见38:31）.

textCards.body: 这一句的希伯来文措辞非常罕见，因此很难翻译。如果像《和修》的做法，译作"朱鹭"和"雄鸡"，那么这一句的意思很符合下一段落（38:39-40:2）的语境。智慧和聪明这两个词的组合，使《和》的译法更加可能，即译作：怀和心，因为这两者被认为是管理人的行动，并运用从上帝而来的智慧。

textCards.body: 译作河马的希伯来词通常指牛，但至少在另一处用到这个词时很可能是指河马。在这段经文中，这个词几乎全部指河马，将16-18节的描述视为诗歌体的夸张乎法。然而有些人提出，参考后面的描述，这里可能是指某种神话怪兽.与鳄鱼（41:1，《和修》"力威亚探的描述平行。

textCards.body: 摇动尾巴如香柏树，"尾巴"是雄性生殖器的常用委婉说法。考虑到这里对动物的解剖性描写，本节应该这样解释。力量常与生殖能力相连。在中世纪，河马被视为淫荡和罪的象征。大腿的筋，在希伯来经文中，〝筋字没有出现在其他地方。一些古抄本（见亚兰文《他尔根译本》、拉丁译本）以其意指"舉丸"，与第一句的解释一致。

textCards.body: 鳄鱼（《和修》"力威亚探，这段经文所描述的动物可能是鳄鱼；有些解经家认这是一种神话中的生物，代表被上帝创造的大能所征服的各种力量（见3:8及注释）。然而，这段经文的重点是：不论这里是指哪种有能力的受造物，它都是上帝创造的一部分，并且在他大能的统管之下（见诗74:14注）。

textCards.body: 在复兴之后.约伯的兄弟姐妹和其他朋友到他那里，都为他悲伤安慰他，恢复了约伯先前所哀叹的损失（参19:13-19）。这原是三个朋友的初衷（见2:11），然而约伯最终所得到的安慰，主要是透过他与上帝之间的成熟的关系（见42:6），以及上帝在那些先前尊敬他的人面前，为他洗雪冤屈（见"导论：文学特征"。

textCards.body: 上帝为了让约伯清楚明白他的恩典，他温和地责备和审问约伯。我厌恶自己，即我意识到自己话语的无知。上帝的怜悯在约伯谦卑的态度中进一步显明，他在尘土和炉灰中终于得享关系和好的安慰，这是他的朋友没有给他的。译作懊悔的希伯来词，与2:11朋友想要"安慰"约伯的词，具有相同的词根。因此，本节也可译作："我厌恶自己，在尘士和炉灰中得安慰"，这样翻译是可取的，因为它不仅与约伯在整卷书中寻求安慰的主题相符合（见"导论：文学特征"，也与上帝的宣告一致，即约伯对他的议论是正确的（42:7）。

textCards.body: 我的怒气向你…发作，上帝的怒气是向提幔人以利法和另外两个朋友（书亚人比勒达和拿玛人琐法：见2:11）发的。这事与以利户所做的形成对比，他粗暴地假定自己代表上帝发言（36:2），并向约伯和三个朋友发怒（见32:2-3）。议论我不如我的仆人约伯说的是，约伯的话确实显示出极度的痛苦和沮丧失望，但上帝没有就这些话定约伯为有罪。这可能是因为约伯从未丧失来到上帝面前的迫切希望，并且他的话是这个盼望的见证。

textCards.body: 我因悦纳他，就不按你们的愚妄办你们，朋友得到的启示对他们而言是何等可悲的讽刺：他们曾经非常确信地沩智慧辩护一直在反驳约伯的"愚妄"，但到后来，他们竟然发现自己完全错了。这个结论也刻画出上帝的怜悯和约伯的敬度：在约伯需要安慰并且应该得到安慰的时候，他的朋友却给他带来了更多的苦难，但现在约伯有机会为他们代求。藉着为朋友代求，约伯反映出上帝的属性（如：不轻易发怒，并有丰盛的慈爱和怜悯），并显明了他自己已经得到的怜悯。藉着为朋友代求，约伯继续担当他曾为家人忠心履行的代求者的角色（见1:5）。

textCards.body: 恶人在自己命运逆转的时候哑口无言。罪孽之辈必塞口无言，类似的句子在对话中出现过好几处，也出现在《诗篇》（见诗107:42）。以利法在5:8-16中暗示，约伯应该重新审视他的境遇变迁，明白这代表着上帝公正的心意（见17节）。
textCards.searchText:
约伯记 5:16 研修本注释：恶人在自己命运逆转的时候哑口无言
恶人在自己命运逆转的时候哑口无言。罪孽之辈必塞口无言，类似的句子在对话中出现过好几处，也出现在《诗篇》（见诗107:42）。以利法在5:8-16中暗示，约伯应该重新审视他的境遇变迁，明白这代表着上帝公正的心意（见17节）。

textCards.body: 在一系列威胁人生命的灾祸中（19-26节），除了饥荒、战争、野兽，以及各种危及牲畜、家庭和个人的祸患（如疾病、灾难等）外，以利法还提到口舌之害。以利法使用数字（"六次 就是七次"来特别强调最后一样：如果约伯承认他的状况是上帝的管教，那么他就会脱离祸患，得享"寿高年迈"（26节）。

textCards.body: 溪水（《和修》"河道"）指干涸的河床。在岩石地带的低地或裂谷，由于骤雨或融雪，水会顺着山坡而汇聚，涌流而下。沙漠中的旅行者无法携带足够的水，他们依靠雨水或者融化的雪，但这些水源在烈日之下会很快枯竭。

textCards.body: 约伯在回应每个朋友时，基本上都是先对朋友说话（6:1-7:10），然后继续哀叹并向上帝诉苦（12-21节）。本节的三个平行陈述（我 ）显示，约伯从回应以利法转为回应上帝。约伯在指称上帝时，从第三人称（如6:9的"他"，原文直译：他伸手将我剪除），转为第二人称（如7:12的"你）。同样，约伯在第一次回应比勒达（10:1-2）和顼法（13:13-17）时，也有类似的重要转变。

textCards.body: 约伯问上帝是否看他像洋海或大鱼一样广大或有力.因为他觉得自己所遭受的苦难与自己的力量不相称。在古代近东的文献中，海洋常被描述为或者拟人化为受造界的一种威胁，需要予以约束或征服（见26:12，38:8-11）。"洋海"（Yam）和"大鱼"（Tannin）都是迎南宗教中的著名形象：Yam拥有极大的力量，与巴力争夺至高权柄，而Tannin则是混沌怪物之一（参诗74:13：赛51:9）。

textCards.body: 我厌弃性命，约伯在9:21使用同一个动词再次宣告他拒绝他的生命。当约伯完全面对上帝的奥秘时，他又用同一个动词拒绝自己所说的话（42:6"我厌恶自己"。这些经节多次重复这个动词，用来凸显约伯态度上的转变。他终于意识到，他自己不可能完全看到或明白他个人生命和境遇的意义，倒不如放弃这份执着。

textCards.body: 拉哈伯是一头野兽的名字，取自非以色列民族的神话，像力威亚探一样（见3:8：参7:12）；这些名字似乎特别代表混沌的力量（见赛30:6-7注）。在早些时候，约伯曾经请求那些能够毁灭力威亚探的力量，从天地中抹掉他出生的那一天（参3:8）。约伯在这里宣告，只有上帝才能征服这些权势。不论约伯认为自己有多正直，他也不敢奢望能够与上帝争辩。

textCards.body: 我虽有义，也不回答他，约伯在这里陈述他的论点（20节重复）：他的朋友把他们的神学观点应用到他和他的境遇上，某种程度上使他陷人困境。约伯的朋友辩称，上帝是公义的，他不离弃完全人，因此约伯的境遇表明：他隐藏了一些应该悔改的事情。约伯认同上帝是公义的，但他觉得自己没有申诉的机会，他并没有犯下朋友所假定的罪。

textCards.body: 在希伯来原文中，译作显我为弯曲的动词形式，主语可能是"他"（指上帝，参《和修》）或"它"（指约伯的口），二者的意思相同。如果约伯的口证明他错了，是因为约伯认为上帝会从他的话中找到缺失之处。当约伯说："我虽完全"（及21节），他是在毫不知情的情况下呼应了上帝在引子中对他的描述（1:8.2:3）。

textCards.body: 《约伯记》是《圣经》中内容最高深的书卷之一，作者佚名，读者只能从字里行间对他略知一二。作者喜欢引用箴言来阐述他的论点，如："按我所见，耕罪孽，种毒害的人都照样收割"（4:8）；"人生出来必遭遇患难，如同火星飞腾"（5:7）；"空虚的人却毫无知识；人生在世好像野驴的驹子"（11:12），因此他肯定可以算为"智者"之一（参箴24:23）。虽然约伯的故事发生在以色列境外的东南方地区（乌斯与以东有关，有可能是本书的背景，参2:11,6:9；哀4:21），但作者是希伯来人，非常熟悉《希伯来圣经》（参以下论述）。《约伯记》作者游历广泛，知识渊博，阅历丰富。他懂得星座（9:9,38:31），谈论气象（38:22-38），熟悉精细的采矿工序（28:1-11）。他描绘蒲草船从水面上掠过的情景（9:26），谈论在沼泽地生长的各种植物（8:11-19）。他曾观察鸵鸟、老鹰、山羊、河马、鳄鱼和战马（39-41章）。正如所有智者一样，他善于广泛运用自然界的事物作类比，以解释和捍卫道德真理。从词源学角度来看，"约伯"这个名字和希伯来文的"敌人"有关，可能是指约伯对上帝的态度，或是他对苦难的回应。这名字也可以是"的父亲在哪里？"这句话的缩写形式。但这是难以确定的，因这名字的真正意思，在最早期的拉比文学注释家中已经失传了。然而，这名字也出现在《圣经》之外的文献中：在巴珊的阿马尔那泥版（约主前1350年）上，约伯是亚斯他录王子的名字；在一份埃及文献中，约伯是一个巴勒斯坦长官的名字（约主前2000年）；在乌加列的一份文献中，这名字又见于皇室成员名单之内。

textCards.title: 约伯记 导论：作者

textCards.body: 本书没有其他参考史料，可以让人确定其写作时间或背景。自古以来就有许多有关《约伯记》写作时间的讨论，《巴比伦他勒目》记载了本书作者的可能人选，包括从列祖时期的某个人物到摩西，或从巴比伦被掳归回的其中一人（《巴比伦他勒目论最后一道门》15a）。书中的英雄人物被赋予列祖时期的背景，呈现出真实可靠的细节和特色，这使许多解经家认为这卷书应该年代久远，甚或是远至亚伯拉罕时期的作品。除了本书之外，《圣经》中最早提到约伯的是《以西结书》。先知以西结提到三位义人的典范：挪亚、但以理和约伯（结14:14,20）。但究竟以西结是通过《圣经》还是其他渠道知道这三个人物，实在无从得知；但以理是最明显的例子，因为在以西结时期，《但以理书》根本尚未成书（至少按照某些学者的观点）。如果以西结是从《圣经》书卷中知道约伯，那就表明《约伯记》属于犹大百姓被掳之前的作品。有人尝试从《圣经》中的神学发展，来估计《约伯记》的成书时间。《约伯记》曾经被视为对申28章的一种详尽阐述（传统称为米示大），或者从民族的苦难问题（正如以赛亚所描绘的）去探讨个人的苦难。然而，以"神学发展"作为论述基础是难以站得住脚的，因为它假设人可以确切地描述这些主题是如何随着时间的推移而发展的。《约伯记》作者直接把《希伯来圣经》（如：诗8:4；参伯7:17-18）融入自己的文字中，有时候甚至直接引用经文（如：诗107:40；参伯12:21,24）。无论是词句的精确重复，或是把《圣经》中的思想加以重申应用，都表明作者曾经接触这些著作，虽然不能肯定这些作品当时的面貌。因此，基于《约伯记》探讨的神学问题，以及书中引用的经文，有人认为本书大约是以西结时期的作品，然而这是一个没有把握的结论。作者运用了大量后期的希伯来文词汇，使得写作日期更难确定，但较为人接受的是在被掳期间（主前587-主前538年）或被掳结束以后（主前538年后）。

textCards.body: 《约伯记》所关注的是在拥有至高主权的上帝面前的信心问题。上帝可信吗？他对世界的统治是否美善和公正？约伯直率地宣称上帝待他不公（19:6-7）。与此同时，约伯肯定他的"敌人"事实上就是他的支持者，并将为他洗雪冤屈。本书从一开始就指出，人类受苦的原因对人类来说往往是个奥秘。事实上，约伯的受苦是来自撒但在天庭对他的指控，但究竟约伯后来是否知道他受苦的真正原因（大概是不知情），作者没有跟读者交代。《约伯记》颇有讽刺意味的事实是：对人类来说，上帝是那么地接近，但同时又是那么地遥远。约伯一方面抱怨上帝时刻紧盯着他，使他不能轻松地咽下唾沫（7:19），另一方面他又觉得上帝难以触摸，认为人不可能寻见上帝（9:11）。上帝虽然时刻关注人类，但对人类各种苦恼的提问，他并不总是给予回应。与此同时，约伯的朋友没有带给他实际的帮助。他们前来"安慰"他（2:11），但最后称他们为"愁烦的安慰者"，竟以"错谬"（《和修》"空话"）来"安慰"他（21:34）。这些朋友代表着一种过于简单化的"正统"观点，对智慧传统的误解，认为人的祸患全都是由过犯招致的惩罚。他们给约伯的"安慰〞信息大都建基在这上面，因此呼吁约伯要认罪悔改。对读者来说，约伯朋友的做法可以作为借鉴，以避免对那些在困苦中的人贸然说出类似的话。令人讶异的是，上帝没有因约伯的话而责怪他，反而肯定他说得"正确"（见《和修》：42:7）。整体来说，本书旨在阐明，即使我们无法完全了解上帝做事背后的原因，但我们在极大的痛苦中依然应该保持对上帝忠心。再者，约伯内心深处的困惑和质疑，并没有激怒上帝。

textCards.body: 《约伯记》为秉持不同信仰观点的所有人处理了一个共同的问题，即使对于那些视世界为自然力量的结果，并以预设的方式运行的人来说，也同样适用。《约伯记》作者特别向那些相信一位拥有位格的创造主的人说话；根据这位创造主的自我启示，他的名字称为耶和华（上帝）。《约伯记》只关乎上帝与人之间的事情，是写给那些在一个充满苦难的世界中、为至高上帝的公义而挣扎苦思的人。作者没有提供神义论的答案，以捍卫上帝的公义。对于这个目的来说，约伯的朋友自始至终都是陪衬，他们的智慧可以代表那些想要解决这个难题之人的努力，但在作者看来，这些努力全属徒然。上帝也宣告，约伯的朋友全都错了（42:8）。以利户的介入加深了问题的探讨，但他也不是约伯所寻求的调解人。作者关注的是，人在遭受苦难时信心的得胜，就这个目的来说，他所描绘的英雄成功了。约伯可以胜利地宣告："我知道我的救赎主活着。"（19:25）显然，约伯自始至终坚定地爱和信靠那位像仇敌一样攻击他的上帝。无论在民族还是个人层面，苦难问题自古就有。因此，我们无需认为是某个民族的独特处境引发作者写了《约伯记》，但这种联系可能适用于《申命记》和《以赛亚书》等书的写作。作者有意不将自己的作品局限在某个特定的处境中。以色列的智慧作者在他们自己的思想和世界观背景中写作。虽然他们也会使用其他文化的谚语和作品，尤其是埃及的，但他们的写作是向着同胞（最初的读者），表达他们自己的信仰。同时，他们认为他们的思想适用于所有时代的所有人："万民哪，你们都当听这话一切的居民，无论上流下流，富足贫穷，都当留心听！我口要说智慧的言语；我心要想通达的道理。"（诗49:1-3）本书有意选取一个非以色列人做英雄，在诗歌部分尽量避免使用以色列人对上帝的称呼（从第3章开始），直至上帝说话（除了伯12:9中引用赛41:20），也没有特别提到任何历史事件，为要传达这是一个普世适用和永恒的视角。

textCards.title: 约伯记 导论：写作目的、缘起和背景

textCards.body: 如上所述，本书作者是以色列人，他指出约伯居住在乌斯，即在以色列境外。约伯的敬虔（1:1）是以色列传统智慧的典型，他呼求耶和华的名字（1:21）。但他和亚伯拉罕后裔之间的关系却仍是个谜。故事的背景大概是列祖，即亚伯拉罕、以撒和雅各的年代。结14:14，20（参这两节经文的注释）将约伯与另外两位古代人物相提并论，加强了这一印象。另外，本书对上帝惯用的称呼，"上帝"（希伯来原文'Eloah，即'Elohim的单数名词）及"大能者"（希伯来原文Shadday），似乎也更适合出3:14，6:3之前的时期（耶和华上帝的名字只出现在首尾的1-2、38-42章，中间部分的唯一例外是12:9）。以西结先知将约伯与挪亚和但以理并列，暗示他认为约伯是一个真实人物。雅各大概持相同意见，他说："那先前忍耐的人，我们称他们是有福的。你们听见过约伯的忍耐，也知道主给他的结局，明显主是满心怜悯，大有慈悲（雅5:11）。与此同时，为了加强表达效果，作者添加了许多文学元素。究竟约伯与他的朋友是否真是以如此优美的诗句对答，对作者来说这并不重要。

textCards.title: 约伯记 导论：约伯及其背景

textCards.body: 在上帝与他百姓交往的历史中，忠心的人无辜受苦是一个经常出现的问题。《约伯记》提醒上帝的百姓，有一个敌人（撒但）昼夜控告他们。约伯朋友的无知提醒我们，在任何时候都要记得，无论什么情况，我们所能看见的都像盲人摸象一样，只是看到大图画的一小片。这也教导我们，在面对人生的困惑时应当信靠和顺服上帝，忠心的人要以温柔、谦卑（罗12:15）之心互相支持和鼓励。虽然耶稣的死亡和复活并没有解除这种困惑，但为约伯对"救赎主"的盼望奠定了坚实的基础（19:25-27）。关于"救恩历史"的解释，见《圣经文集》中的《〈圣经〉概述》和《〈旧约〉中的救恩历史：为基督预备道路》。

textCards.title: 约伯记 导论：救恩历史概要

textCards.body: 作者以散文形式描述敬虔的约伯遭遇灾祸与复兴，构成全书的框架（1:1-2:13，42:7-17）。读者在这里遇到一位无可指摘的人物——约伯，他平安稳妥和富足昌盛的生活被突发的悲剧中止，在他毫不知情的情况下，上帝与撒但谈到约伯（参1:6注）。1:9提出的问题"约伯敬畏上帝，岂是无故呢？"似乎是这段散文部分主要关注的问题，在本书的结论部分，这个问题得到了充分而令人满意的解答。夹在首尾两段散文中间的，是一长段诗歌体裁的对话，让读者聆听故事中重要角色的心声。约伯的独白（3、28、29-31章）夹在约伯与他三位朋友——以利法、比勒达和琐法（参2:11）三轮激烈的辩论之间。三位朋友的对话，从开始时候的相信约伯正直（参3:23-25,6:4）.同情他（4:2-5），发展成埋怨约伯自义（27章），到最后转变为严厉地指控约伯（22章）。由始至终，核心似乎都在以利法提出的问题："必死的人岂能比上帝公义吗？人岂能比造他的主洁净吗？"（4:17；参约伯的提问：9:2,比勒达的提问：25:4）结果，约伯自己也不断地自我反省，并且在朋友们一连串过于简单化的道德指控中，越来越勇敢地为自己的无辜辩护。约伯渴望上帝为他洗雪冤屈，渴望有一位仲裁者可以为他伸冤（参9:33，16:19-21，19:25-27）。由于读者早已瞥见约伯受苦背后的属天奥秘，因此自然期待辩论过后，上帝要向辦论者宣告他们的错误，并拯救约伯脱离苦境。然而，这一幕现在还未出现。相反，一个新的角色登场，他是整卷书中唯一有希伯来名字的人：以利户（意为"他是上帝"或可能是"耶和华是上帝"），他的父亲是巴拉迦（意为"愿上帝赐福"或"上帝已经赐福"；参32:6）。在一连六章的篇幅（32-37章）中，以利户同时责备约伯和他的朋友。读者应该怎样理解他的介入？解经家对此有非常不同的见解。从经文本身看，可以发现几个明显的因素。（1）以利户为约伯提供了他渴求的小型"仲裁""，却非约伯寻求的答案，但他确实指向正确的方向。（2）先前的对话诉诸传统与观察，以利户引入了人受灵感动的概念（32:8,18-20）；有人认为这是对智慧讨论的一种先知式的公开回应。（3）以利户找到并引证辩论双方都有不足的地方（33:1,34:2）；另外，以利户预期上帝自己将表明立场（38-42章）。（4）也许更重要的是，以利户重新定位整场辩论，将重点由约伯和人类的道德问题，缓慢却坚定地转移，呼吁人专一转向上帝，他才是人类确实和盼望的根基（参36:22-23，37:14-24）。同时，以利户可能高估了自己的贡献（32:6-10）。他和约伯的三个朋友一样，根本不知道事件背后的真相（1-2章），某些论证与约伯的朋友重复。此外，当上帝最后说话时（38:1），他似乎完全不理会以利户（另参42:7）。以利户可能坚持他论证的核心部分有某些是正确的，但他如何运用这些真理，以及他有关约伯的结论，明显与上帝对约伯所说的话有很大差别。从文学层面来看，以利户的发言是藉着推迟揭晓最终的结局来制造悬念。最后，上帝在旋风中显现（38:1,40:6），正如约伯一直相信他会如此行（参9:17a）那样。"耶和华讲话"（38-41章）并不是直接回答约伯的提问，而是揭示约伯受苦难背后的真相：上帝早在那里。上帝现在显然与约伯在一起，就在他的苦难中与他同在。读者从本书的引子部分就可以知道事件的"真相"（1-2章），这有助于辨明约伯与朋友之间的对答内容，并明白本书的结局。一方面，约伯力陈自己无辜有合理的地方，然而从他的整个对答可以看到他性格的发展；另一方面，约伯朋友的有些言论也是有理有据的。尽管这些言论是"正统"的，但就他们对约伯的指控来说，读者可以作出明智的判断。因此，当上帝最后称赞约伯，并指示他的朋友请求约伯为他们代求（42:7-9）时，其实读者从开篇的两章中早已知道其中的原因了。本书最重要的关键词是"安慰"'，书中指示在哪里可以找到真正的安慰。在2:11，约伯的三个朋友来安慰他；在6:10，约伯因为没有违弃那圣者的言语而得着安慰；在7:13，约伯声称上帝不容许他的床安慰他。在15:11，以利法声称他带来上帝的安慰；在16:2，约伯称他的朋友是"愁烦的安慰者"；在21:34，约伯更断言他们企图以空话来安慰他。在21:2，约伯讽刺地向朋友提议，只要他们听他的倾诉就是对他的安慰"了。关键在42:6：如今上帝开口说约伯可以说他是"在尘土和炉灰中得着安慰"。在42:11，约伯的亲戚和朋友来安慰他，可能具有讽刺意味：约伯已经在上帝无法测度的智慧的异象中找到了他所需要的安慰。

作者以散文形式描述敬虔的约伯遭遇灾祸与复兴，构成全书的框架（1:1-2:13，42:7-17）。读者在这里遇到一位无可指摘的人物——约伯，他平安稳妥和富足昌盛的生活被突发的悲剧中止，在他毫不知情的情况下，上帝与撒但谈到约伯（参1:6注）。1:9提出的问题"约伯敬畏上帝，岂是无故呢？"似乎是这段散文部分主要关注的问题，在本书的结论部分，这个问题得到了充分而令人满意的解答。夹在首尾两段散文中间的，是一长段诗歌体裁的对话，让读者聆听故事中重要角色的心声。约伯的独白（3、28、29-31章）夹在约伯与他三位朋友——以利法、比勒达和琐法（参2:11）三轮激烈的辩论之间。三位朋友的对话，从开始时候的相信约伯正直（参3:23-25,6:4）.同情他（4:2-5），发展成埋怨约伯自义（27章），到最后转变为严厉地指控约伯（22章）。由始至终，核心似乎都在以利法提出的问题："必死的人岂能比上帝公义吗？人岂能比造他的主洁净吗？"（4:17；参约伯的提问：9:2,比勒达的提问：25:4）结果，约伯自己也不断地自我反省，并且在朋友们一连串过于简单化的道德指控中，越来越勇敢地为自己的无辜辩护。约伯渴望上帝为他洗雪冤屈，渴望有一位仲裁者可以为他伸冤（参9:33，16:19-21，19:25-27）。由于读者早已瞥见约伯受苦背后的属天奥秘，因此自然期待辩论过后，上帝要向辦论者宣告他们的错误，并拯救约伯脱离苦境。然而，这一幕现在还未出现。相反，一个新的角色登场，他是整卷书中唯一有希伯来名字的人：以利户（意为"他是上帝"或可能是"耶和华是上帝"），他的父亲是巴拉迦（意为"愿上帝赐福"或"上帝已经赐福"；参32:6）。在一连六章的篇幅（32-37章）中，以利户同时责备约伯和他的朋友。读者应该怎样理解他的介入？解经家对此有非常不同的见解。从经文本身看，可以发现几个明显的因素。（1）以利户为约伯提供了他渴求的小型"仲裁""，却非约伯寻求的答案，但他确实指向正确的方向。（2）先前的对话诉诸传统与观察，以利户引入了人受灵感动的概念（32:8,18-20）；有人认为这是对智慧讨论的一种先知式的公开回应。（3）以利户找到并引证辩论双方都有不足的地方（33:1,34:2）；另外，以利户预期上帝自己将表明立场（38-42章）。（4）也许更重要的是，以利户重新定位整场辩论，将重点由约伯和人类的道德问题，缓慢却坚定地转移，呼吁人专一转向上帝，他才是人类确实和盼望的根基（参36:22-23，37:14-24）。同时，以利户可能高估了自己的贡献（32:6-10）。他和约伯的三个朋友一样，根本不知道事件背后的真相（1-2章），某些论证与约伯的朋友重复。此外，当上帝最后说话时（38:1），他似乎完全不理会以利户（另参42:7）。以利户可能坚持他论证的核心部分有某些是正确的，但他如何运用这些真理，以及他有关约伯的结论，明显与上帝对约伯所说的话有很大差别。从文学层面来看，以利户的发言是藉着推迟揭晓最终的结局来制造悬念。最后，上帝在旋风中显现（38:1,40:6），正如约伯一直相信他会如此行（参9:17a）那样。"耶和华讲话"（38-41章）并不是直接回答约伯的提问，而是揭示约伯受苦难背后的真相：上帝早在那里。上帝现在显然与约伯在一起，就在他的苦难中与他同在。读者从本书的引子部分就可以知道事件的"真相"（1-2章），这有助于辨明约伯与朋友之间的对答内容，并明白本书的结局。一方面，约伯力陈自己无辜有合理的地方，然而从他的整个对答可以看到他性格的发展；另一方面，约伯朋友的有些言论也是有理有据的。尽管这些言论是"正统"的，但就他们对约伯的指控来说，读者可以作出明智的判断。因此，当上帝最后称赞约伯，并指示他的朋友请求约伯为他们代求（42:7-9）时，其实读者从开篇的两章中早已知道其中的原因了。本书最重要的关键词是"安慰"'，书中指示在哪里可以找到真正的安慰。在2:11，约伯的三个朋友来安慰他；在6:10，约伯因为没有违弃那圣者的言语而得着安慰；在7:13，约伯声称上帝不容许他的床安慰他。在15:11，以利法声称他带来上帝的安慰；在16:2，约伯称他的朋友是"愁烦的安慰者"；在21:34，约伯更断言他们企图以空话来安慰他。在21:2，约伯讽刺地向朋友提议，只要他们听他的倾诉就是对他的安慰"了。关键在42:6：如今上帝开口说约伯可以说他是"在尘土和炉灰中得着安慰"。在42:11，约伯的亲戚和朋友来安慰他，可能具有讽刺意味：约伯已经在上帝无法测度的智慧的异象中找到了他所需要的安慰。
导论：文学特征

textCards.body: 一、引子：约伯的品行以及他受试炼时的环境（1:1-2:13）（一）约伯的纯正（1:1-5）（二）第一次试炼（1:6-22）1.天上的挑战（1:6-12）2.失去家人和财产（1:13-19）3.约伯的宣告和信心（1:20-22）（三）第二次试炼（2:1-10）1.天上的挑战（2:1-6）2.约伯的受苦与认信（2:7-10）（四）来安慰约伯的朋友（2:11-13）二、对话：约伯、他的受苦和他在上帝面前的地位（3:1-42:6）（一）约伯：痛恨自己的生日（3:1-26）1.引言（3:1）2.约伯诅咒他的生日（3:2-10）3.约伯渴望安息（3:11-19）4.约伯哀叹他的受苦（3:20-26）（二）三个朋友与约伯：约伯在上帝面前可能是义的吗？（4:1-25:6）1.第一个循环（4:1-14:22）a.以利法：必死的人在上帝面前怎能成为义呢？（4:1-5:27）b.约伯：生命是虚妄的（6:1-7:21）C.比勒达：圣贤的智慧（8:1-22）d.约伯：人在上帝面前怎能成为义呢？（9:1-10:22）e.琐法：悔改（11:1-20）f.约伯：挑战朋友的"智慧"（12:1-14:22）2.第二个循环（15:1-21:34）a.以利法：约伯的话定了自己的罪（15:1-35）b.约伯：受苦者的指望（16:1-17:16）C.比勒达：恶人受刑罚（18:1-21）d.约伯：我的救赎主活着（19:1-29）e.琐法：恶人终必死亡（20:1-29）f.约伯：恶人兴旺发达（21:1-34）3.第三个循环（22:1-25:6）a.以利法：约伯有罪（22:1-30）b.约伯：上帝是隐藏的（23:1-24:25）C.比勒达：未被回答的问题（25:1-6）（三）约伯：上帝的大能、智慧的所在和纯全的道路（26:1-31:40）1.上帝道路的奥秘和威严（26:1-14）2.申明纯正和盼望冤屈得雪（27:1-23）3.智慧何处寻？（28:1-28）4.约伯的生命道路（29:1-31:40）（四）以利户：受苦是一种管教（32:1-37:24）1.引言：以利户及其怒气（32:1-5）2.年轻人的声音（32:6-22）3.约伯的听讼人（33:1-33）4.呼吁智慧人（34:1-37）5.约伯在上帝面前有什么理？（35:1-16）6.上帝的怜悯与威荣（36:1-37:24）（五）挑战：上帝回答约伯（38:1-42:6）1.第一个挑战：认识宇宙万物（38:1-40:2）2.约伯的回应：沉默（40:3-5）3.第二个挑战：认识公义和大能（40:6-41:34）4.约伯的回应：降服（42:1-6）三、结尾：约伯的冤屈得雪、代祷和复兴（42:7-17）（一）上帝责备三个朋友（42:7-9）（二）上帝复兴约伯（42:10-17）
